data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: Scandal-hit banking quango in row over redundancies
data.description:
A scandal-hit banking quango is embroiled in a row after using controversial redundancy clauses to block former staff from launching legal claims.

Open Banking Limited (OBL), established in 2016 to encourage banks to share data, has attempted to muzzle former employees during a recent round of redundancies by prohibiting them from taking action against the group once they leave.

The redundancy contracts include clauses restricting individuals from pursuing grievances after termination. Former staffers are also prevented from submitting subject access requests to obtain their personal data and from raising complaints with the Information Commissioner’s Office.

It is unclear whether such terms are enforceable, but OBL maintained in a statement that its historic settlement agreements were legal.

It is understood that at least one former employee is proceeding with an employment tribunal claim because of concerns about the way the redundancy process at OBL has been handled.

OBL was founded a decade ago to encourage savers to switch their bank accounts and boost competition among lenders.

It is funded by nine of the biggest banks in the UK, and regulators at the Competition and Markets Authority are tasked with overseeing the venture.

A spokesman for OBL said that it had “updated the terms of our settlement agreements” and was “confident that our approach complies with employment law”.

It is not the first time that the little-known watchdog has found itself under scrutiny.

In 2021, an independent report found a “toxic culture” of bullying and intimidation prevailed at the quango, which led to the resignation of its chairman.

The Telegraph revealed details at the time of the mistreatment of staff at OBL, which included on-the-spot sackings, verbal abuse, sexism and a culture of cocaine use.

A spokesman for OBL said that, since 2022, a “wholesale cultural overhaul has taken place” at the regulator with “full senior leadership change, supported by robust internal governance, processes and procedures”.

The revelation of the redundancy clauses comes as OBL will soon transition to a new entity which will be funded differently and overseen by the Financial Conduct Authority instead.

The big banks that pay for OBL to operate have criticised the cost of funding it in the past, and last year the group’s chairman said the future entity would have a “fairer and more equitable funding model”.

A spokesman for OBL added: “From time to time at OBL, as with all organisations, there is business change. As a consequence, regrettably, there are a small number of redundancies.

“As part of our redundancy process, OBL may offer individuals a voluntary standard settlement agreement. These agreements are entered into on a voluntary basis by both parties as a means of concluding an individual’s employment. We note that the majority of our small number of redundancies are concluded in this manner.”

The CMA declined to comment.

data.title: The towns where the taps ran dry
data.description:
On a grey Tuesday in East Grinstead, a little girl runs along the pavement to the redbrick town library. She stands at the automatic glass doors waiting for them to open, but nothing happens. A moment later her mother arrives. The woman looks at the sign that reads “library closed today”, then tells the child she will not be able to borrow any books after all.

Like almost every business or public building in this West Sussex town, the library is shut as a result of South East Water’s failure to keep the taps running.

The company’s bosses might just about be forgiven if this were a one-off. But it is the second officially declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent, still facing disruption after weeks of problems.

South East has repeatedly fallen short on its most basic duty. In 2023, 6,000 households in Kent and Sussex went without water when supplies at South East ran out. In 2022, thousands of people spent Christmas with no water due to leakages.

The company, which serves 2.3 million customers, is now well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from basket-case Thames Water, leaving its licence to operate under threat.

Businesses on the brink

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of South East Water, who took home £515,000 last year including a £115,000 bonus, was hauled in front of a parliamentary committee earlier this month. He had been summoned to answer questions on the crisis that hit Tunbridge Wells in December and ruined Christmas for 24,000 homes and businesses.

The spa town made an unlikely backdrop for a water crisis. From the 17th century, Tunbridge Wells’s mineral waters drew bathers and wealth.

But in the 21st century, water dealt an economic blow. Pub and bar owners in Tunbridge Wells told The Telegraph they had lost ruinous amounts of money.

“In four days we lost between £15,000 to £20,000. We are really struggling,” Bianca Omer, the manager of the cocktail bar Rocca said.

The bar survived. But Tudor Price, the chief executive of Kent’s chamber of commerce, believes that pubs, restaurants and bars in Tunbridge Wells “potentially lost about £20m of revenue” as a result of the outage.

Some are now on the brink. With many pubs already threatened by the Chancellor’s business rates raid, lengthy closure may deliver the final blow for some in Tunbridge Wells.

More alarmingly, a kidney treatment centre, 19 care homes, and two hospitals were left without water too.

‘Flying blind’

Debate has raged over whether the crisis in Tunbridge Wells was foreseeable. Hinton, perhaps unsurprisingly, reckons not.

At his parliamentary hearing he told Alistair Carmichael, the no-nonsense MP for Orkney and chairman of the environment select committee, that the whole thing was a bolt from the blue. In fact, because the crisis came as such a shock, Hinton insisted, he’d give himself “eight out of 10 in terms of response”.

The A-grade mark, as Carmichael put it, raised eyebrows among committee members. But when Marcus Rink, the boss of the Drinking Water Inspectorate, delivered his verdict, the benefit of the doubt evaporated.

“It shouldn’t have been a surprise,” Rink said. The water treatment works at nearby Pembury hadn’t just gone down for a few hours – it had been failing for some time, he explained.

“The start of the incident, in the opinion of my inspectors, was Nov 9.” With the Pembury plant the only water treatment works serving Tunbridge Wells, its continued good health is essential to the area.

Rink said South East was “flying blind”. The plant lacked electronic monitoring, relying on manual observations that missed failing filters.

Despite prior regulatory enforcement notices and missing micro-filtration units, the company had ignored weeks of visible deterioration, eventually leading to a totally avoidable system collapse.

The picture for South East became worse as Rink laid out more details. The company had been notified that there were issues with the treatment works a year previously. Testing had been intermittent, and the specialists monitoring the facility had all knocked off on Friday afternoon before returning on Monday morning. Nov 9 was a Sunday.

If MPs have been determined to hold Hinton to account, the same cannot be said of South East Water’s non-executive directors. Other managers have been sent out to face the music, among them a flustered Neil Hudson, South East’s head of water quality.

“What would happen to people if they do drink it?” a local BBC news reporter demanded to know after residents were advised to boil their water before using it.

“Any sort of normal, healthy individual will probably see no impact whatsoever. If anyone drinks the water and starts to feel unwell, our advice would be to speak to your GP,” Hudson said.

Asked by MPs why he had dodged the media, Hinton claimed journalists would not have asked relevant questions. They would have focused on his salary and South East Water’s finances, he complained.

When he finally came out of hiding, Hinton told ITV that he was “an operational CEO” and as such had spent the crisis participating in “traditional CEO activities”.

Henry Tuffnell, the young Labour MP for Pembrokeshire who sits on the environment committee, pointed out that for an “operational CEO” Hinton seemed to be remarkably short of operational detail.

Tuffnell tells The Telegraph: “You can’t say you were all over the detail, and therefore didn’t have time to do any media, if you weren’t over the detail.”

Even as Hinton faced MPs, back in Tunbridge Wells a second major incident was already in the offing. Still recovering from the pre-Christmas outage, locals were cut off again on Saturday.

Round two, according to South East, was due to “recent cold weather and a subsequent breakout of leaks and bursts”. This was no minor blip. Within days, 30,000 homes and businesses across Kent and Sussex had no water at all.

‘We have had enough’

In East Grinstead, four schoolboys kick a yellow football against the wall. They are the only people in the town who seem to think the outage is a good thing. “It’s a day off,” one of them offers cheerfully.

In Waitrose, almost all of the water has gone from the shelves except for a few bottles of San Pellegrino and some strawberry-flavoured Volvic. There was a water station in the car park the previous day, but it closed after 90 minutes.

East Grinstead council said: “We were not contacted directly by South East Water at any stage during the recent water supply disruption in the town. To the best of the council’s understanding, local knowledge was not utilised when setting up the initial water distribution stations.

“The town council regrets that it was not involved from the outset, as early engagement and local knowledge could have helped ensure a smoother, safer, and more coordinated response for the town’s residents.”

Local knowledge is clearly not South East Water’s strong suit. In December the company began its comedy of errors in Tunbridge Wells with the opening of a water station in Tonbridge, seven miles away.

For those affected, it’s hard to see the funny side. Gary, who lives homeless in East Grinstead, and had just two bottles of water left, told The Telegraph he was “scared”. Like many in the town, he wondered: “What is this all about?”

Jonathan Hawker, a Tunbridge Wells resident who set up the campaign group Dry Wells Action, says: “We can’t wash, flush toilets, do the dishes, wash clothes, top up boilers – all the normal stuff you take for granted – because of this abject failure by South East Water.

“We have had enough. We will now fight for our right to water. Tunbridge Wells is no longer disgusted. We are absolutely furious.”

Heavy hitters

South East’s board is now facing serious questions. Chris Train, the chairman, has been no more forthcoming about appearing publicly than his chief executive.

Described on the company’s website as bringing “a lot of experience”, he is also chairman of Sellafield, which in 2024 was fined for serious cyber security failings. The Commons public accounts committee criticised the nuclear power plant last year for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns”.

After Hinton’s select committee mauling, Train offered a review by one of the non-executive directors.

Committee chairman Alistair Carmichael responded: “Members of the press and public would be forgiven for seeing Mr Train’s announcement of a further review of the Pembury failure as an attempt to buy themselves time, to hunker down until this storm blows over.

“They also assert that their review will be ‘independent’. It stretches the meaning of ‘independent’ when their review is to be conducted by a member of its board, supported by its staff. What was the investigation done by the Drinking Water Inspectorate if it was not an independent review?”

Hinton’s furious phone call

Desperate Tunbridge Wells residents have called for the Army to be brought in. Though that may sound a little over the top, there is logic to the request.

Hawker says: “The Army are logistics specialists who are used to dealing with challenging situations. We need experts who can manage logistics in emergency situations.”

South East Water officials can’t even manage a basic task like carting bottles of water from one place to another, he argues.

If Hinton has been the baddie, Mike Martin, the Liberal Democrat MP for Tunbridge Wells, has emerged as something of a local hero. He has had his sleeves rolled up throughout the process and repeatedly taken Hinton to task. This resulted in Hinton phoning Martin one evening during the eye of the storm to give him both barrels.

In his evidence to the environment committee, Martin said Hinton had accused him of politicising the crisis, asking: “How can you sleep at night?” The chief executive admitted to Carmichael that the telephone tantrum had not been his “finest moment”.

South East is more used to getting away with it.

As well as the four major shortages customers have endured on Hinton’s watch, South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since Hinton took over. In 2023, it had the dubious accolade of recording the most interruptions and outages in England and Wales.

Its performance is getting worse, not better. In 2025, just 13 of it 38 annual targets were met – a little more than a third. Among the areas where it fell short were leakages, supply interruptions, water taste and odour, customer satisfaction and value for money. The previous two years, South East scored 15 – equivalent to less than 40pc. In 2022, it ranked positively on 24 of the 38 goals.

The company has suggested that its systems would be much better if only it had been able to raise customers’ bills, a familiar refrain in the water industry.

Yet it already has. Bills went up by 20pc just last year, meaning the average customer now pays £294 a year. It is also one of only five companies pushing to lift bills above the figure set by the regulator over the next five years.

Ofwat also gave short shrift to South East’s most recent investment plan. The company’s performance commitments were “of low to moderate ambition and do not go far enough in some cases”, the regulator said last April.

When it comes to “water supply interruptions, per capita consumption, repairs to burst mains and biodiversity”, the targets South East Water proposed “remained among the lowest across the sector”, Ofwat added.

South East responded by accusing the watchdog of being “unfair, disproportionate and punitive”. The company boasts of having “60 teams repairing leaks reactively and 86 leakage technicians and analysts searching for and finding leaks proactively”. More than 25,000 leaks were repaired in 2024, it says.

For any customers who feel they’re not getting value for money, the company points out: “The average drinking water bill now costs 81p per day, less than the cost of a second-class stamp.”

A spokesman said: “Our priority is improving South East Water in the future. We are strategically focused on our business plan and committed to delivering the infrastructure investment needed. This will improve our resilience as we face the challenges of climate change and population growth.”

It adds that Pembury’s water treatment works at the centre of the Tunbridge Wells crisis is due a full upgrade: “In advance of this full refurbishment, a number of improvements and changes are being made. Some of these are complete, and others are still ongoing, all of which are in line with the regulators’ timescales.”

Finances are underwater

South East’s financial performance is no less wretched than its operational performance. The company has failed to post a profit for the last three years, and though pre-tax losses roughly halved to £19.7m last year, they total £130m since 2022.

Weighed down with £1.3bn of borrowings – a figure that has steadily increased every year under Hinton – the cost of servicing those debts regularly eclipses the company’s profits. In the last five years, more than £400m has gone out of the door on debt costs and finance charges alone.

A further £168m has been paid out dividends, £136m of it in the form of a regulator-approved repayment of an internal loan to its parent company in 2020. South East Water is ultimately owned by a consortium comprising Australian and Canadian funds and NatWest’s pension fund.

In the two-year period from 2020 to 2022, the amount that South East paid out in dividends and debt interest surpassed what it spent upgrading infrastructure.

In 2024, the company revealed that it needed further cash to stay afloat. Ten months later, it received a £200m equity injection from shareholders.

Yet none of this has stopped Hinton receiving hundreds of thousands of pounds in pay and bonuses every year. Total earnings since assuming the top job in 2020 stand at an estimated £2.3m. Last year, he was paid a basic salary of £307,000, a £115,000 bonus and received a further £35,000 in pension contributions and other benefits.

A spokesman for South East said: “South East Water remains committed to a remuneration framework that supports a performance culture, and recognises success but does not reward poor performance.

“The bonus scheme is evaluated by the company’s independent non-executive director-led remuneration committee. The executive directors are not part of this committee and have no say in their remuneration.

“We can confirm that no bonus payment was made for operational performance for the year 2024-25. Details of all payments made can be found in our annual report on our website.”

‘Out of control’

Yet pinning the fiasco on a lack of investment when executives’ backs are against the wall doesn’t wash with Tunbridge Wells MP Mike Martin.

“Quite clearly, corporate governance has broken down,” he said. “We are now in a situation where a major public utility is out of control – failing its customers, risking public health and treating Parliament with contempt.”

If the board won’t step up, Martin thinks the utility’s bosses have to get involved. NatWest’s pension scheme owns 25pc of South East Water.

“NatWest Group Pension Fund will use its influence as a minority shareholder to direct South East Water’s board to ensure these issues are fully resolved,” it said in response to a letter from Martin this week.

The regulator has been spurred to action too. On Wednesday evening, the Government finally showed some mettle by calling on Ofwat to review South East Water’s licence to operate.

In an uncharacteristic show of speediness, on Thursday Ofwat announced an investigation into whether the supplier had met its obligations and offered support to those affected. Ofwat sceptics point out the regulator has been investigating South East since 2023.

There are local suspicions that Labour have, to a degree, let the crisis escalate because Tunbridge Wells and East Grinstead will never turn red. Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform UK, told The Telegraph that’s overthinking it. He said: “The problem is utter management incompetence at South East Water. My hunch is cock-up, not conspiracy.”

Yet the crisis is ongoing, and the inquisition is just getting started. By the water station at East Grinstead sports club, where the cars are backed up as far as you can see and people are pushing shopping trolleys full of bottles back home, a little boy on a bike asks if you can drink the water from the pond.

data.title: This British tech champion had an £18bn vision for AI. Now it’s in turmoil
data.description:
As Sir Keir Starmer unveiled a £31bn technology pact to coincide with Donald Trump’s state visit last September, one AI deal buried within the flood of investments raised eyebrows.

The Government announced that a previously unknown company, AI Pathfinder, would invest £1bn to build a data centre in Northamptonshire.

“Nobody had even heard of them before yesterday,” said one bemused industry source at the time.

Within days, AI Pathfinder revealed the extent of its ambitions, with £18bn of data centre investments across England and Scotland, exploiting Labour’s efforts to deliver an AI-fuelled infrastructure blitz.

Martin Bellamy, AI Pathfinder’s chief executive, featured in another government briefing in November on the launch of a new “AI growth zone”.

“AI Pathfinder is driving economic growth and social value through sovereign AI,” he said.

“This is a vote of confidence in the UK’s vision: delivering world-class capability while keeping sensitive data firmly on British soil.”

Yet just a few months on, AI Pathfinder is in turmoil.

The data centre developer’s website now features a placeholder image and nearly all of its staff have left.

AI Pathfinder’s £18bn vision is at risk of unravelling amid a bitter legal battle with Patrick Hughes, a waste management tycoon and one of the tech company’s biggest investors.

The dispute has already led to the High Court freezing AI Pathfinder’s assets, while a recent judgment also noted how the company had sought restructuring advice from Alvarez & Marsal and Teneo.

Bellamy told The Telegraph that his company’s plans to build a vast data centre in Scotland remained under way despite the dispute.

However, the unfolding saga threatens to become a cautionary tale for the developers, entrepreneurs and officials seeking to cash in on the AI gold rush.

Data centre frenzy

Global technology giants have pledged to spend more than $500bn (£370bn) this year on AI infrastructure alone to meet growing demand for computing power.

This frenzy has since spread to Britain, where there has been a surge in applications to build data centres.

Some £43bn in data centre investments have been announced in the UK since 2023, according to research by Oxford Economics.

A clutch of property developers with limited tech credentials has pledged to spend billions of pounds developing brownfield sites into AI hubs.

AI Pathfinder’s rise ...

Against the backdrop of this spending boom, AI Pathfinder’s proposals would have made it one of the biggest data centre operators in the UK – aside from hyperscalers like Microsoft.

The data projects were intended to cover two sites, in Northamptonshire and Ayrshire.

While the company was still in its infancy, the team behind it had plenty of experience developing major property projects, albeit not in the AI sector.

For example, Bellamy, 56, was also the founder and chairman of Salamanca Group, a property development group.

The business had previously led work on Gravity, a campus in Somerset set to include a £4bn gigafactory, and a former Britishvolt campus in Blyth, Northumberland.

Salamanca also previously owned Marina Port Vell in Barcelona, a superyacht hub once frequented by Russian billionaires such as Roman Abramovich.

A few years ago he and his wife Darina also hit the headlines over development plans near their Cotswolds home.

Revealing its plans last September, AI Pathfinder said it intended to build a major site near Chelveston, Northamptonshire, and ramp up its project in North Ayrshire, at a business campus known as i3, with up to 1.5 gigawatts of capacity.

This would have made it one of the biggest data centre sites in Europe. In total, AI Pathfinder said it intended to build two gigawatts of capacity in Britain.

Bellamy claimed in September that the UK was at an “inflection point where slow or non-adoption of AI becomes an existential risk for the Government.”

He added: “Sovereign capacity is becoming not just strategic, but essential.”

The AI business’s board included Lord Johnson, a former investment minister under the Tories.

... and fall

However, despite its ambitions, there were few public details about AI Pathfinder’s funding.

Behind the scenes, seed funding for the business had been provided by Hughes, who invested £27.5m in AI Pathfinder and its parent company, Mulberry Limited, according to court filings.

Hughes, 55, has almost no public profile, but Companies House filings show he has helped to run a series of businesses in the construction and waste industries.

The latest is Valencia Waste Management, which was previously part of Viridor and controlled by private equity giant KKR. It owns 44 landfill sites around the UK and recorded revenues of £138m in 2024.

Hughes appears to have started ploughing tens of millions of pounds into AI ventures over the past year – including AI Pathfinder.

He is also the main shareholder in a separate UK AI business, Carbon3.ai, which has promised to invest £1bn in data centres around the UK. It is planning to build a network of AI data centres powered by landfill gas from Valencia’s sites.

However, his investment in AI Pathfinder has drawn the most attention owing to his dispute with Bellamy.

According to a High Court judgment, Hughes initially accused Bellamy of reneging on an agreement to stump up £10m in funding.

Bellamy denies there was any such arrangement and has said the legal claim is without merit.

This then led to a further clash in December, when the two entrepreneurs offered to buy each other out of AI Pathfinder.

Hughes escalated the spat in January by suggesting that he could appoint receivers to seize control of the business.

The allegations have recently culminated in Hughes freezing the company’s assets, based on claims that parts of the business were being sold below value “behind his back”.

The dispute is ongoing.

Meanwhile, as AI Pathfinder navigates its legal battle, a team of the company’s former executives appears to have launched a separate venture, Sovereign AI.

The 20-strong team includes some of AI Pathfinder’s former executives, with the company hailing plans for its own AI data centre network, working with US tech giant Palantir.

Sovereign AI insists it is not connected to Bellamy or Hughes. Despite this, Hughes has also launched a legal claim against Sovereign AI.

A spokesman for Sovereign AI said: “Sovereign AI has no connection whatsoever to Martin Bellamy or Patrick Hughes.

“Neither party is a shareholder, a director, nor has any interest in, or other involvement with, the company. We are fully focused on building a world-class sovereign AI champion.”

Regardless, Bellamy says AI Pathfinder remains committed to its North Ayrshire project, which is “one of the UK’s most important sites for an AI data centre campus”.

He says his team have “worked tirelessly” with the local council and Government to ensure the region is a “priority for power delivery, planning and government focus”.

He adds: “A revised one gigawatt power offer is expected towards the end of February, with a planning application due to be lodged in the summer.”

How the project plays out will inevitably be vital for Labour, which is betting that a multibillion-pound data centre blitz will help the UK capitalise on AI.

Bellamy also believes his team can forge a British champion for AI infrastructure, but he has a fight on his hands to prove it.

data.title: Killer motorists should be treated like the murderers they are
data.description:
April 25 2007 is etched forever in my mind. It was a beautiful day, and summer was just around the corner. My twin sister had given birth to a baby boy, and my 65-year-old mother, Elizabeth, had come to stay with me in Mortlake, south London. We planned to go to the Chelsea and Westminster Hospital to visit the new baby.

My husband walked my two eldest children, Barnaby and Sapphire – who were six and four at the time – to school and then hopped on a train to work. My mother, my two-year-old daughter, Pollyanna, and I set off to catch the bus to the hospital.

My mother, who lived in rural Shropshire, loved London buses and much preferred them to the train.

We walked, with Pollyanna in her pram, the short distance from our house to the local bus depot. As we approached, Pollyanna climbed out of her buggy and held my mother’s hand.

This was when my life changed irreversibly. I turned around, and all I could see was a monstrous, red Metroline bus approaching at a ferocious speed. Instead of turning into the depot, it drove on to the pavement, aiming straight for us. It happened so fast – almost like a bomb going off.

My legs were trapped under the bus, and I was like the jam in the middle of a horrid sandwich – bus, me, wall. Blood was everywhere. I slipped in and out of consciousness. Out of one eye, I saw my darling, precious, beautiful mother slipping away. She did not survive.

Out of my other eye, I saw my little daughter about 20 ft away. She had been flung through the air, and her right leg was hanging off. I was trapped and could not help her. I shouted for my mother so loudly. I wanted her to answer me, but she did not. She could not.

The crash quickly flooded the news as my husband – The Telegraph’s Christopher Hope – received a call at work to tell him what had happened.

As he made the terrible journey from his office in Victoria to Mortlake, he did not even know if we were alive or not. Four surgeons spent seven hours trying to save Pollyanna’s leg, but they could not, and she had it amputated.

I suffered severe degloving in my leg (where skin and soft tissue is torn from the muscle or bone underneath), among other injuries. However, knowing that I would never see my mother again was the most unbearable wound.

The bus driver who hit us was found guilty of one count of death by dangerous driving and two counts of grievous bodily harm. He was sentenced to four years in prison, which is simply not long enough. Often, only aggravating factors like being under the influence of drink or drugs result in a longer sentence, but it is rare that criminal drivers get more than 12 years behind bars.

On average, four people die every day on UK roads, and many dozens more suffer life-changing injuries. It is no wonder that victims of road crime – like me – feel utterly let down by the UK’s criminal justice system.

Metroline would not speak to me when I rang up their head office. I felt upset, hopeless and angry. So, in February 2014, I set out on a mission – for road crime to be treated as real crime, and for victims and their families to be treated with kindness and compassion in navigating the criminal justice system.

I met Boris Johnson, who was the mayor of London at the time. He said he was very sorry for what had happened to my family. He asked me whether Transport for London (TfL) had apologised, and I said no.

He told me that must change. A few weeks later, my husband and I met with TfL and Metroline. They said they were very sorry. I said that “sorry” must hold meaning, and things began to change from then on.

The Sarah Hope Line was launched in February 2016. It is an incident line that offers confidential support to people involved in, or affected by, life-changing incidents on the TfL network. We have provided to more than 500 families what TfL did not give to us: trauma counselling, assistance with funeral costs and arrangements, and short-term accommodation for family members. Next Monday will mark our 10-year anniversary.

While the line supports families of the bereaved, we still have not yet changed the attitude within government that road crashes are “just one of those things”. This Government should spend much more on policing our roads, so that criminal drivers can be caught. We need to issue immediate fines and driving bans so motorists are deterred from driving dangerously.

Road crime and the damage it causes cost the taxpayer billions of pounds every year – it strains the NHS, criminal justice system and our emergency services. Fatalities, alone, cost the NHS billions every year. That money should be spent on policing our roads and preventing such devastation.

Perpetrators think they can get away with causing death by dangerous driving because sentences do not reflect the severity of the crime. Change must start now. We must no longer be the forgotten victims.

The Sarah Hope Line is on 0343 222 5678, Monday to Friday, 8am-6pm. For more details, go to https://tfl.gov.uk/travel-information/safety/incident-support-service

data.title: Train lines ‘sabotaged’ near Winter Olympics
data.description:
Italian police suspect sabotage after three incidents including a fire on railways caused major delays for those travelling to the Winter Olympics.

Investigators have not ruled out arson carried out by anarchist groups, similar to what is thought to have happened in France at the Paris 2024 Olympics.

Saturday’s blaze, on the first full day of the games, was “presumably ‍deliberate” but had not been claimed, a spokesman for the police said.

Transport police and the anti-terrorist Digos force were investigating on site near the northern city of Bologna, the spokesperson said.

Local media reported that several cables were cut near Bologna’s central station, causing disruptions to high speed trains, cancellations and delays of up to 90 minutes.

Damage was reported on the Bologna-Padua line, where a crude explosive device was found. An electrical sub station caught fire on the Bologna-Ancona line in Pesaro, shortly before 6am on Saturday.

The fire hit a track switch and severed electrical cables used to detect train speed, causing delays ‍of ‌up to two hours for high-speed, Intercity and regional services at one of Italy’s busiest rail hubs.

The ‍fire targeted the line between Bologna and Venice, but also caused knock-on problems for traffic between Bologna and Milan, ⁠and ‌routes to ‍the Adriatic coast.

Milan is a co-host for the Winter Olympics alongside Cortina, which is accessible via train from Venice.

Italy’s state-owned railway Ferrovie dello Stato (FS) said that despite the disruption, trains were still running. 

Rail traffic was slowly returning to normal on Saturday.

The Italian ministry of transport said: “These actions of unprecedented gravity in no way tarnish Italy’s image in the world, which the games will make even more convincing and positive. 

It added that “the serious episodes of sabotage that occurred this morning near Bologna station and in Pesaro, which caused serious inconvenience to thousands of travellers, are worrying and are similar to the acts of terrorism that occurred in France a few hours after the opening ceremony of the Paris Olympic Games.”

At the 2024 Summer Olympic Games staged ⁠in Paris, saboteurs struck France’s TGV high-speed train network in ‌a series of pre-dawn ⁠attacks across the country, ‍causing travel chaos ahead of the opening ceremony.

Hundreds of thousands of passengers were affected by the disruption caused by what was called coordinated “acts of sabotage” by the then prime minister, Gabriel Attal.

Far-Left militants were blamed for the attack at the time. Iran denied being behind the sabotage after reports that Israel had warned France of an Iranian plot to derail the games.

data.title: AI means NatWest is hiring a new breed of banker
data.description:
Young people should consider studying tech if they want a career in banking, the boss of NatWest has suggested.

Paul Thwaite, the chief executive, said banks were looking for “very different” graduates from those hired in the past amid the coming boom in artificial intelligence (AI).

He said data scientists, software engineers, architecture planners and “prompt engineers” – people who design instructions for AI tools – were the new breed of banker he was seeking to hire.

“The profile of graduates that we recruit today, and we’ll do in future years, is very different, for example, to what we were recruiting four or five years ago,” he said.

NatWest hires around 250 graduates every year, spread across tech, commercial banking, risk management and financial crime.

As a student, Thwaite studied management science and chemistry at the University of Manchester, then joined Santander’s graduate scheme before switching to NatWest.

In a sign of its growing tech focus, the bank has invested £1.2bn across the group in AI and simplification in an effort to cut costs.

AI is already being employed in areas such as customer service and it said using AI to handle complaints had led to saving around 90,000 hours per year.

Overall, the bank reported pre-tax profits of £7.7bn over the year, up a quarter from 2024 and the highest since before the financial crisis that forced NatWest into a government bailout. Shares in the bank were down by 1.8pc by mid-morning.

Mr Thwaite received £6.6m in pay over the year, up from £4.9m in 2024, making him the best-paid boss of the bank since 2010. NatWest also announced plans for a £750m share buyback programme.

NatWest unveiled a £2.7bn deal to buy wealth manager Evelyn Partners on Monday as it looks to diversify away from traditional lending.

The acquisition is the bank’s largest since its £49bn deal for ABN Amro in 2007, a takeover which precipitated its near collapse and state bailout during the financial crisis.

Shares in NatWest have fallen around 10pc since news of the acquisition, amid concerns that the bank might have overpaid.

However, Mr Thwaite said he believed that the drop in the share price was to do with the wider economic and political uncertainty.

“It’s been a pretty volatile week for a whole host of sectors. You had some of the political disruption at the start of the week and economic disruption, not just domestically, but also internationally,” he said.

“I’m not worried because it’s the acquisition of a strategic asset,” he added. “It’s very attractive from a growth perspective.”

data.title: Britain plots Visa rival over fears Trump could pull the plug on payments
data.description:
Britain is accelerating plans to launch an alternative to Mastercard and Visa amid growing fears Donald Trump could pull the plug on the global payments system.

The UK’s largest banks are plotting a new British platform to handle the 50 billion payments made every year after concerns were raised about Mr Trump’s ability to switch off the current system.

Lenders including Barclays, Natwest, Lloyds, Santander and Nationwide are set to meet on Thursday for the first time to develop a blueprint for the scheme, the Guardian reported. The forum is chaired by Vim Maru, head of Barclays UK.

The new system will be used to keep the economy afloat if the President decided to interfere with the payments network amid fears over his attacks on the UK and Europe.

Banks and regulators are also hoping the scheme will cut Britain’s reliance on US financial infrastructure, which underpins much of the world’s payment schemes.

“If Mastercard and Visa were turned off, it would send us back to the 1950s,” one executive told the Guardian, referring to the period when cash was used for the majority of payments.

The President’s strident tone on defence matters as well as his threats over Greenland have prompted the UK and EU to step up plans to lessen their reliance on US systems and infrastructure.

This includes accelerating plans for a rival payments system, which is dominated by US cards giants Visa and Mastercard. Both handle about 95pc of all card transactions in the UK.

Mr Trump has shown a willingness to use an array of financial weapons to achieve his aims, raising fears that the payments system could be weaponised in the future.

In January, he threatened the UK and other EU nations with 10pc tariffs if they did not back his claim to Greenland. Although he backtracked on the plan, it has raised alarm from policymakers who fear the President could throttle payments to achieve his aim.

The Treasury originally unveiled plans for the “next generation of UK retail payments infrastructure” last year, with the Bank of England establishing the Retail Payments Infrastructure Board taskforce to develop the system.

Britain’s banks are also working on blueprints for implementing the Visa and Mastercard rival, which is called DeliveryCo. UK Finance, the industry trade body, is orchestrating the plans.

A Visa spokesman said: “Visa remains committed to providing UK consumers and businesses with access to innovative, secure digital payments with the highest levels of resilience and reliability. 

“We welcome the industry progress on account-to-account payments in the UK. 

“We believe competition between multiple solutions, supported by a level playing field, will deliver choice, innovation and economic growth in the UK.”

Mastercard said: “The payment industry has never been more competitive. 

“Mastercard has been fully invested in the UK for decades, delivering consumers and businesses with a wide range of convenient, simple and secure ways to pay and get paid. 

“We remain committed to drive commerce here at home and across the globe by helping businesses of all sizes grow and meet the needs of their customers.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Reeves accused of putting vital South West rail route at risk
data.description:
Rachel Reeves has been accused of putting South West England’s main rail route at risk by limiting maintenance spending while promising billions for Northern projects.

Fears are mounting that a sea cliff could collapse on to the line in Devon, cutting off Plymouth and the whole of Cornwall, after recent storms saw a sinkhole near the railway in Dawlish.

The cliff in question had been earmarked for £400m of earthworks after a 2014 collapse closed the line for eight weeks, costing the local economy £1.2bn.

However, the plan was abandoned after Labour sought to cut costs across the UK’s rail network. Instead, ministers suggested the cliff would be monitored for signs of movement. 

Storm Ingrid, which battered Devon two weeks ago, also saw waves destroy sections of a sea wall protecting the tracks at Dawlish, closing the line for two days.

Rebecca Smith, Tory MP for South West Devon, said the damage had stoked concerns that spending cuts had left the lifeline route exposed to potential disaster.

She said: “With all the tightening of the budget by the Chancellor, she has taken away contingency funding. So if there were something that needed an emergency reaction, the implication is that the money isn’t actually there.

“If you get the right kind of freakish storm, who knows what could happen. The question for me is, should this not be something that is in capital funding on a year-on-year basis?”

After Ms Smith asked in the Commons whether funding could be guaranteed for unplanned resilience work, James Murray, the chief secretary to the Treasury, said that budgets allow ministers to respond to emergencies.

He said: “All individual departments must recognise that unexpected pressures will come along; they need to prepare for them and make sure they have robust plans to respond.”

Ms Smith, a member of the transport select committee, said the Government’s reluctance to fund work on the cliff stood in contrast to its backing for the £45bn Northern Powerhouse Rail project, for which Ms Reeves last month announced £1bn of preparatory funding.

She said: “Why do they want to take a gamble when they are investing billions in other parts of the country? Why do we need a marginally faster rail system in the North when in the Southwest, if this went again, we’d be cut off for, I don’t know how long?”

Ms Smith also raised doubts about the level of monitoring that Network Rail is carrying out on the cliff, which is protected by the railway from the sea but prone to becoming sodden with surface water.

She said: “Are they monitoring it or just draining things? We need answers if they are not prepared to put the investment in to fix it.”

Martin Wrigley, Liberal Democrat MP for Newton Abbot, which includes Dawlish, said that had Storm Ingrid hit when seas were higher, the damage could have been much more severe.

He said the Department for Transport (DfT) should grant Network Rail money to undertake a study into a long-term solution for the cliff.

He said: “Monitoring is no good because the last collapse was a single, catastrophic failure, almost a tectonic shift of part of the cliff which came down on to the railway.”

Mr Wrigley said the damage to the Dawlish sea wall was also concerning, having affected areas on either side of a new section installed after the 2014 collapse.

The DfT said work carried out after the 2014 events held up well in the recent storms, with damage limited to a low wall between the coastal path and the railway. 

It said funding had been provided for a drainage trial and a range of advanced monitoring equipment, including tilt sensors that would provide a quick response if triggered.

Work will continue with Network Rail to assess the investment needed to ensure the line’s resilience and to develop any longer-term solutions.

data.title: Heathrow boss: Airport isn’t crowded... foreigners just walk the wrong way
data.description:
The chief executive of Heathrow has said the airport is not crowded because foreigners just walk the wrong way.

Thomas Woldbye said people complain the airport is too busy when in fact people are just “crashing into each other”.

This is because European travellers are used to walking on the right, while the British walk on the left.

Mr Woldbye told an Aviation Club UK event on Thursday: “One of the reasons people think [Terminal 5] is crowded – it’s not, actually. But yes, people are in the wrong place.

“Problem is that all the British people keep to the left and normally Europeans keep to the right. And they do that in both directions. So we can be crashing into each other and I see that from personal experience.

“We just [need to] make sure that everybody going this way keeps going left, and [those] going this way keep to the right,” he added, gesturing with his hands to illustrate the different directions.

The humorous aside came after the Danish-born airport boss warned earlier this week that Heathrow could lose its coveted spot as Europe’s busiest airport to rivals such as Amsterdam or Paris unless a third runway is approved for construction.

Mr Woldbye said on Tuesday: “We remain Europe’s largest airport but latest figures show we may lose that position in 2026 and we cannot keep driving growth for the UK economy without more capacity.”

When asked what he thought of the Government’s position on the third runway, he said it was broadly supportive. But he added: “We don’t have long-term regulation, which is clear in terms of us building a business case.”

Last year, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, gave the go-ahead to Heathrow’s preferred plan to build a third runway over the top of the M25 motorway, a scheme likely to generate controversy over its cost and complexity.

The third runway scheme, which will see a 3,500-metre strip built to the north-west of Heathrow’s existing site, is currently estimated to cost around £49bn.

Included in the controversial plans are a scheme to build a 66,000-space car park at a cost of £2.6bn, as revealed by the City AM financial newspaper last summer.

The airport boss was also criticised on Thursday for raising drop-off fees paid by drivers to £7 – and threatening an £80 fine for motorists spending longer than 10 minutes saying goodbye to their loved ones.

He said in response to the criticism: “So when we make profits on all parts of the business, rather than just running the airport, that actually goes to reducing the charge [applied to passengers’ tickets].

“Whenever we make less money ... passengers are actually paying [for] that,” he added.

“We have an incentive to make as much money as we can, on the side, from anything that actually reduces the charge for passengers.”

Heathrow’s charges are regulated by the Government. The airport pays for its infrastructure by imposing a charge on airline passengers’ tickets.

Airlines, including Virgin Atlantic and British Airways, have set up a pressure group called Heathrow Reimagined, which calls for “urgent and fundamental reform” of this funding system.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Elon Musk bids to build swarms of drones for US military
data.description:
Elon Musk’s SpaceX is bidding for a secretive contract to build swarms of voice-controlled drones for the US military.

The Pentagon has launched a $100m (£74m) competition to develop an AI bot that can be used to translate voice or written commands from soldiers to a fleet of drones.

Mr Musk’s SpaceX is one of the companies pitching for a share of the work, Bloomberg reported.

OpenAI, the developer of ChatGPT, is also said to be working with US autonomous vehicles business, Applied Intuition, on a rival bid.

The US military’s Defence Innovation Unit last month confirmed it was seeking bidders to develop an “autonomous vehicle orchestrator”, an AI system that can be used by ordinary soldiers in the field to command “autonomous systems at the fleet level” that can be used to “overwhelm our adversaries”.

Portable drones have proven to be vital weapons on the frontlines in Ukraine and are used for long-range surveillance and lethal attacks while operated by pilots holed up miles away.

Drone swarms are seen as crucial hardware for soldiers in future conflicts. They could be used to swamp enemy defences or guard friendly troops.

Lt Gen Frank Donovan, the director of the US military’s Defence Autonomous Warfare Group, said the system would “allow humans to work the way they already command – through plain language ... not by clicking through menus or programming behaviours”.

He added: “We must ensure the human – the ethical decision maker – always maintains a clear understanding of what the system is doing and why.”

Mr Musk’s SpaceX is already a key defence contractor for the US government, providing rocket launches and communications through its Starlink satellite business.

Efforts to bid for the military contract come after Mr Musk merged his SpaceX business with xAI, another company he owns and which controls social media platform X and the AI bot Grok.

‘Pandora’s box’

Mr Musk has previously warned against the prospect of AI-powered weapons. In 2017, he signed a letter warning that AI warfare threatened to be a “Pandora’s box” and in 2018 cautioned that AI was “far more dangerous than nukes”.

Silicon Valley AI labs have jumped at the opportunity to scoop up lucrative US defence contracts and offer up their tools for military use. In 2024, OpenAI, the developer of ChatGPT, scrubbed a ban on military use of its AI technology from its company policies.

Earlier this month, OpenAI said it would be offering a customised version of ChatGPT to the Pentagon. A spokesman said: “With the proper safeguards, AI can help protect people, deter adversaries, and prevent future conflict.”

OpenAI has a $200m contract to work with the US department of defence’s AI office. Mr Musk’s xAI, Google and AI lab Anthropic all have similar deals to provide their AI bots to the US military.

However Anthropic, the developer of the Claude chatbot, is reported to have clashed with the Pentagon over the military’s plans for its technology.

The US has been urging companies to allow their AI to be deployed for “all lawful purposes”, something Anthropic has been resisting, Axios first reported.

SpaceX, OpenAI and Applied Intuition were contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain’s hapless water watchdog is asleep on the job again
data.description:
Just when you thought things couldn’t get any worse for the parched residents of Tunbridge Wells and surrounding towns, here comes Ofwat to the rescue.

With some households in the region facing their second bout of water shortages in the space of a few weeks at the hands of South East Water, some must have been praying for a miracle. Yet the comically late arrival of Britain’s regulator is the equivalent of sending in Dad’s Army.

Responsibility for the fiasco that has unfolded ultimately resides with South East Water’s equally inept, overpaid chief executive Dave Hinton and the board of nodding dog directors who have somehow seen fit to allow him to remain in post.

But for an enforced and humiliating appearance in front of a parliamentary committee of senior MPs, Hinton has been completely missing in action throughout this debacle.

Nor is this catalogue of failures a one-off. Under its current boss, South East Water has a thoroughly woeful track record of failing in its basic duty to keep the taps running for 2.3 million customers in Kent, Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Berkshire.

This is the second officially-declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent still facing disruption after weeks. Thousands of households were also hit by shortages in 2022 and 2023.

As my colleague Patrick Galbraith and I point out in a separate piece for The Telegraph, South East Water is well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from industry basket case Thames Water, which really does take some doing given its current sorry state.

Mr Hinton should have been shown the door long ago, but he’s being kept in post by the company’s unforgivably meek independent directors, led by equally absentee chairman Chris Train, who knows a thing or two about companies that fall short.

Train is also the chairman of Sellafield, which was fined for serious cybersecurity failings in 2024. The following year, the Commons Public Accounts Committee (Pac) criticised the nuclear power plant for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns.”

Yet, the idea that Ofwat’s intervention will make much of a difference really is the stuff of fantasy.

Britain is awash with dire regulators who are meant to protect consumers from cowboy firms. The Financial Conduct Authority was branded “incompetent at best, dishonest at worst”, by MPs and peers in 2024.

Meanwhile, Pac found in 2022 that failures at Ofgem had come “at a considerable cost to billpayers” after the collapse of a staggering 29 energy suppliers in less than 18 months.

The only regulator that has shown some bite is the Competition and Markets Authority. And its reward for standing up for consumers? A thorough defanging at the hands of ministers who claimed it had become an impediment to economic growth.

Otherwise, in a crowded field, Ofwat must be a contender for the most ineffective industry policeman of all time. It is a watchdog that has repeatedly demonstrated little, if any, willingness to properly hold the industry to account, as South East knows only too well from considerable experience.

The vast bulk of Britain’s waterways have become a public health emergency, and water shortages are now commonplace – the result of extreme underinvestment in the country’s decrepit pipes and sewers by an industry that has routinely prioritised generous executive pay and investor dividends.

On every conceivable measure and metric, companies have been failing consumers on a grand scale for years.

Ultimately, the buck stops with greedy, irresponsible suppliers and shareholders. Yet, years of regulatory apathy have allowed something as basic and fundamental as the provision of drinking water to be turned into a giant and lucrative experiment in financial engineering at the expense of customers.

On the occasions when Ofwat does act, it’s like watching a snail wake up from a coma.

South East Water is already the subject of a regulatory investigation – one dating back to 2023 that still hasn’t been resolved – so what chance of a quick verdict?

As Mike Martin, the MP for Tunbridge Wells, points out: “It’s obvious that South East Water has breached the terms of the license.”

In the face of Ofwat’s impotence, is it any wonder the company has been getting away with it for ages?

Four major outages have been racked up on Hinton’s watch, and South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since he took over.

2023 was particularly shocking, with the company recording the most interruptions and outages of any supplier in England and Wales.

Meanwhile, South East has failed to meet more than 40pc of its targets every year for the last three years. Last year, just 13 of its 38 annual targets were hit. Unsurprisingly, Ofwat penalties of a few million pounds here and there have had little effect.

Financially, it is a mess, too. Total losses since 2022 stand at £130m, the result of profits being consistently wiped out by the cost of servicing £1.3bn of borrowings. 

At times, debt interest and dividends have eclipsed the money that has been spent mending leaky pipes and other vital infrastructure.

Hinton, meanwhile, has earned nearly £2.3m since taking charge in 2020 and is in line for a 30pc pay rise, plus a £400,000 long-term “service bonus” regardless of performance. Talk about reward for failure.

It is an untenable situation that has long been crying out for a supervisor with real mettle.

Lynn Parker, senior director for enforcement at Ofwat, is right to say that “the last six weeks have been miserable for businesses and households across Kent and Sussex” – but why did it take so long for Ofwat to do anything?

Even now, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that Ofwat only acted because Emma Reynolds, the Environment Secretary, had called publicly for it to step in.

While the regulator has dithered, senior MPs, together with Martin, have done a brilliant job of holding Hinton and colleagues to account.

Ofwat, on the other hand, is hard to take seriously. The threat of penalties, including the annulment of South East’s licence, is somewhat hollow to say the least, given the Government’s plans to abolish Ofwat, the Drinking Water Inspectorate and the Environment Agency, and replace it with a single water regulator responsible for the entire water system.

It is a welcomed move. Long-suffering customers deserve better and the disbanding of the current abject regime is a rare opportunity to create a watchdog with genuine clout. Labour cannot afford to get it wrong.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: BBC mast operator in crisis over terrestrial TV switch-off
data.description:
The company behind Britain’s terrestrial TV and radio network has been plunged into crisis amid uncertainty over a looming digital switchover.

The future of Arqiva, which operates around 1,500 masts used by the BBC and other broadcasters, has been thrown into doubt after its largest shareholder warned its investment in the company would likely be written down to zero.

In an update to the stock market this week, Digital 9 Infrastructure said its stake of almost 52pc in Arqiva is now thought to be worth nothing.

The writedown comes as the BBC and fellow public service broadcasters ITV and Channel 4 are locked in discussions with ministers about when terrestrial TV signals, delivered via Freeview, will be switched off and replaced by streaming.

The broadcasters are keen to push ahead with the switch-off, arguing that they face huge costs to maintain energy-intensive terrestrial signals as viewers increasingly shift to streaming.

Arqiva’s network of masts reaches 98.5pc of the UK population through Freeview.

It includes the 319-metre-tall, Grade II-listed Emley Moor transmitting station, which is the tallest freestanding structure in the UK, and Lancashire’s Winter Hill, which beams TV signals to more than 2.7 million homes in areas including Manchester and Liverpool.

Under current legislation, traditional TV is slated to continue until at least 2034. However, campaigners have called for this deadline to be extended, warning that a hasty switch-off could leave older and vulnerable audiences behind.

The campaign is heavily backed by Arqiva, which has a significant commercial interest in extending the life of traditional broadcast networks.

Exposure to ‘binary events’

Ministers this week kicked off a separate review into whether FM radio signals should also be switched off in the 2030s. The commercial radio industry had previously warned that the sector was being overlooked in discussions about switching off terrestrial masts.

Digital 9 acknowledged that Arqiva was “exposed to several binary events, including government policy and regulatory decisions”.

In an update to the stock market this week, Digital 9 Infrastructure said its stake of almost 52pc in Arqiva, which was once worth £213m, is now thought to be worth nothing.

Arqiva’s tumbling value had already been signalled in November when Australian investment giant Macquarie sold its 26.5pc stake for £16.5m, giving the company a valuation of just £62m.

Digital 9 has maxed out its debt facilities, raising questions over its ability to raise more money in the event of a future crisis.

A spokesman for Arqiva said Digital 9’s valuation of its business related to the “specific to the structure of their investment”.

The added: “Arqiva is a stable business with a healthy order book of existing business (£2.8bn as at 30 June 2025) and excellent targeted growth opportunities.”

Discussions over the future of terrestrial TV have proved to be politically fraught as officials in Whitehall seek to manage the transition to streaming without leaving audiences behind.

A report by Exeter University found that while 95pc of UK homes will use TV services delivered over the internet by 2040, 5pc will still rely on terrestrial TV. The report found that these people were more likely to be female, older and from lower socioeconomic backgrounds.

In addition to the switch-off, Arqiva is gearing up for the renewal of its broadcasting contract with the BBC, which is expected next year.

It comes as the BBC also enters negotiations over the future of the licence fee, with ministers considering alternative funding models including subscriptions and advertising.

The BBC is grappling with a squeeze on its budget following a freeze in licence fee income and as more households choose not to pay the levy. Corporation bosses yesterday told staff that further cost-cutting of up to £600m is now planned over the next three years.

While ITV and Channel 4 are commercially funded, they are also grappling with a drop in revenues as viewers defect to streaming rivals such as Netflix. ITV is in negotiations over a £1.6bn takeover by Sky, though talks have begun to slow, Reuters reported on Thursday.

Digital 9 warned that its stake is worthless because the value of the investment has fallen below the level of debt it took on to acquire the shareholding from the Canada Pension Plan. Digital 9 insisted there was a “credible pathway to value in certain scenarios”.

Shonil Chande, an analyst at Panmure Liberum, said the fact that Digital 9 had “substantially overpaid” for Arqiva was “no secret”.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Warner Bros gives Paramount a week to submit ‘best and final offer’
data.description:
Warner Bros has given Paramount a week to table its “best and final” takeover offer, raising the prospect of a fresh bidding war with Netflix.

The Hollywood studio, which has already struck an $83bn (£61bn) deal with Netflix, said it had agreed with the streaming giant to give Paramount until Feb 23 to improve on its rival $108bn bid.

Warner Bros said its board continued to back the Netflix deal and said a shareholder vote on the takeover will take place on March 20.

However, the move signals a softening of the company’s position after it rebuffed repeated approaches from Paramount, which is controlled by Larry Ellison, the billionaire Oracle founder.

David Zaslav, the chief executive of Warner Bros Discovery, said the studio was engaging with Paramount to determine whether it could address the “deficiencies” in its previous offers and present an “actionable, binding proposal”.

Warner Bros said the change of course came after a “senior representative” for Paramount told one of its board members that the rival suitor could increase its bid from $30 to $31 per share.

They added that $31 per share would not be Paramount’s “best and final” offer, according to Warner Bros, which is currently valued at $28 a share. Netflix’s initial deal was struck at $27.75 a share.

It comes after Paramount last week launched a fresh attempt to derail Netflix’s swoop on the Hollywood studio behind the Harry Potter franchise.

The US media group offered to pay a so-called “ticking fee” of $0.25 per share to investors every quarter should its deal fail to close by the end of the year.

It also agreed to cover the $2.8bn termination fee Warner Bros would have to pay Netflix if it abandons their agreed deal, as well as $1.5bn in fees linked to Warner Bros’ debt refinancing.

Under the terms of its deal, Netflix has the right to match any improved bid tabled by Paramount.

Risk of monopoly

Paramount, led by Larry Ellison’s son David Ellison, is seeking to highlight the regulatory uncertainty surrounding Netflix’s bid.

The US Department of Justice (DoJ) has launched a competition review into the takeover amid concerns that merging two of the largest US streaming services – Netflix and Warner’s HBO Max – could hand the combined company monopoly power.

Ted Sarandos, Netflix’s co-chief executive, has played down competition concerns.

The company is planning to tell regulators that by bundling Netflix and HBO Max together, it will bring down overall streaming costs for consumers. Nevertheless, Netflix has admitted it could face up to 18 months of regulatory scrutiny.

Paramount, which, unlike Netflix, is seeking to buy Warner Bros’ cable channels including CNN, would likely also face regulatory scrutiny for its proposed deal. However, bosses have argued its approach offers greater certainty.

Netflix said Paramount had “repeatedly mischaracterised the regulatory review process by suggesting its proposal will sail through, misleading WBD stockholders about the real risk of their regulatory challenges around the world”.

It added: “WBD stockholders should not be misled into thinking that Paramount Skydance has an easier or faster path to regulatory approval – it does not.”

Paramount and Netflix have been contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”
