data.title: NHS to pay £3bn more for drugs to escape Trump tariffs
data.description:
The NHS has agreed to pay 25pc more for new medicines to escape Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs on drugs.

The US president unveiled a new pharmaceutical trade deal with Britain on Monday, striking an agreement to impose zero import tariffs on medical products from the UK.

It will see British medicines carved out from sweeping new levies, offering a major boost for one of Britain’s biggest exports to America. In 2024, the UK exported around £6.6bn in pharmaceutical and medical products to the US.

The agreement includes a pledge for the NHS to increase the net price it pays for new medicines by 25pc, which will add around £3bn to the taxpayer’s £12bn annual medical spending bill. This will be phased in.

It follows weeks of uncertainty over the future of the industry. In August, the US president threatened to impose tariffs of up to 250pc on all pharmaceutical imports – including the UK.

In September, he said there would be 100pc tariffs on “any branded or patented Pharmaceutical Product” coming into the US from October 1. At the time, he said companies could get an exemption if they had started building factories in the US.

While the White House has agreed to a spate of country-specific trade deals over the past year, pharma tariffs are expected to fall outside the scope of these agreements.

Under the new agreement, the NHS has also committed to slashing rebates that drug companies have to pay on sales of newer medicines to the health service.

The so-called Voluntary Scheme for Branded Medicines Pricing, Access and Growth (VPAG) is designed to cap the overall NHS drug bill but pharmaceutical companies have complained it has failed to keep pace with inflation.

Additionally, the deal means the UK will cut the rebate rate from 22.9pc to 15pc in 2026.

It is also understood that the Government will increase the threshold that determines which treatments are available on the NHS, boosting the spending cap on certain medicines and paving the way for more cutting-edge treatments to be introduced.

Drug giants have long complained over the decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money, claiming this does not allow them to get the best new drugs into the NHS.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said: “For too long, American patients have been forced to subsidise prescription drugs and biologics in other developed countries by paying a significant premium for the same products in ours.

“Today, the United States and the United Kingdom announce this negotiated outcome pricing for innovative pharmaceuticals, which will help drive investment and innovation in both countries.”

He added: “The Trump Administration is reviewing the pharmaceutical pricing practices of many other US trading partners and hopes that they will follow suit with constructive negotiations.”

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, said the deal would bring new jobs to America.

Mr Lutnick said: “This deal doesn’t just deepen our economic partnership with the United Kingdom – it ensures that the breakthroughs of tomorrow will be built, tested and produced on American soil.”

The White House has previously made clear its frustration over the lack of spending on innovative medicines in Britain. Currently, the UK spends just 0.28pc of its GDP on new medicines, roughly a third of what America does.

Britain previously pledged to review the health service’s drug pricing to take into account “the concerns of the president” under the UK-US trade deal struck in May.

Mr Trump has called for an end to overseas countries “freeloading” on American innovation, singling out Britain for a place where medicine prices are unfairly low compared to the US.

The US ambassador last month said Britain needed to change how it priced medicines or risk more global drug companies scrapping investments in the UK.

Merck, one of America’s largest drug companies, this year ditched plans to open a £1bn research centre in London, while AstraZeneca has paused £200m spending on its Cambridge research facility.

Liz Kendall, the Science and Technology Secretary, said the deal would “enable and incentivise life sciences companies to continue to invest and innovate right here in the UK”.

Chris Boerner, chief executive of Bristol Myers Squibb, said the trade agreement meant the pharmaceutical company will invest “upwards of $500m (£378m)” over the next five years.

Mr Boerner said: “This investment will come across multiple areas of the business including research, development and manufacturing. This agreement is a sign of progress and one that creates an environment conducive to our continued presence in the UK.”

data.title: BBC plots £600m of cuts with fears for jobs and programming
data.description:
The BBC plans to slash more than £500m from its annual budget in a move almost certain to involve job losses and programme cuts.

The public service broadcaster told staff it was aiming to strip out a tenth of its annual costs over the next three years. The BBC’s operating costs stood at just over £6bn last year, meaning the savings would amount to around £600m.

It is not yet clear exactly where the cost savings will be targeted but staff fear job cuts and further cutbacks to output.

Tim Davie, the outgoing director-general, said in a call with staff on Thursday that the cutbacks would affect all parts of the corporation, including its licence fee-funded public service arm and its commercial BBC Studios division.

In a note to staff, Mr Davie wrote: “To stay competitive and relevant, we need to adapt and innovate with purpose and pace.

“Over the next three years, like everybody in our sector, we will need to continue to find savings and move money to where we need it to remain relevant for our audiences.”

It is the latest in a series of demoralising rounds of cuts at the BBC, which had already pledged to strip out £700m by 2028.

The BBC has already wielded the axe in areas including radio and news, with BBC Two’s flagship evening news programme Newsnight slashed to half an hour and the HardTalk interview show scrapped altogether.

The corporation last year said it would spend £150m less on making new shows as it warned rising production costs had left it with an “unprecedented” funding challenge. In 2023, the BBC sparked outrage after cancelling daytime medical drama Doctors after 23 years.

The latest cuts were announced on the same day that a Florida judge ruled that Donald Trump’s $10bn (£7.3bn) case against the BBC will go to trial in February 2027, rejecting the broadcaster’s attempts to delay it.

A BBC spokesman said the cuts were unrelated to the case. They added: “It’s the reality of choices we have to make given ongoing funding pressures.”

The corporation is trying to plug a £500m hole in its finances, triggered by a combination of rising costs and a reduction in the number of Britons paying the licence fee.

The BBC’s licence fee income dropped by 30pc in real terms between 2010 and 2020 after a series of freezes and cuts to the levy. It has also suffered from a steady decline in licence fee payers, with a further 300,000 households ditching the fee last year.

The latest cut announcement came just a week after the Government confirmed that the cost of the licence fee will rise by £5.50 to £180 from April.

The funding crisis comes as the BBC enters into negotiations over the future of the licence fee ahead of the end of the current Charter period next year.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has said she is considering a range of possible reforms, including funding the broadcaster through advertising or subscriptions.

BBC bosses have acknowledged the need for reform but have pushed back against the idea of subscriptions or advertising, arguing that the broadcaster should remain a universal service.

The BBC is expected to submit its proposals for future funding in the coming weeks with other ideas reportedly under consideration including imposing a fee for its radio stations or extending the licence fee to include shows available to stream on services such as Netflix or ITVX.

The United States president’s defamation lawsuit is a further threat to the BBC’s fragile finances. The lawsuit is centred on a misleading edit of Mr Trump’s speech during the Jan 6 2021 riots in an episode of BBC One’s Panorama, which The Telegraph revealed.

The scandal prompted the resignation of both Mr Davie and Deborah Turness, the BBC’s head of news. The BBC has named senior executive Rhodri Talfan Davies as interim director-general, beginning in April, while it searches for a permanent replacement.

A BBC spokesman said: “Over the last three years, we have delivered more than a half a billion pounds worth of savings, much of which we’ve been able to reinvest into our output across the BBC.

“In a rapidly changing media market, we continue to face substantial financial pressures. As a result we expect to make further savings over the next three years of around 10pc of our costs.

“This is about the BBC becoming more productive and prioritising our offer to audiences to ensure we’re providing the best value for money, both now and in the future.”

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Netflix promises cheaper UK streaming in Hollywood takeover fight
data.description:
Netflix is to promise cheaper streaming for British viewers as it seeks to push through its $83bn (£60bn) takeover of Warner Bros.

Netflix is preparing to tell the UK competition watchdog that bundling its streaming service with the soon-to-be-launched HBO Max will result in lower overall costs for viewers.

Warner Bros has confirmed that HBO Max will launch in the UK next month at a starting price of £4.99 per month. It will carry the upcoming Harry Potter TV series alongside HBO hits such as Succession and The Sopranos.

The launch of yet another streaming service will add to costs for British households seeking to view the widest range of programmes.

However, Netflix will argue that a combined package of its own service and Warner’s HBO Max would cost less than two separate subscriptions if its deal goes through.

The emphasis on cost savings is understood to form part of Netflix’s defence as it gears up for scrutiny by the Competition and Markets Authority.

HBO Max already operates in the US, and Ted Sarandos, Netflix’s co-chief executive, told a US Senate committee earlier this month that people who subscribed to both would receive a “meaningful discount”.

It is not yet clear whether Netflix will maintain the two services as separate apps or whether HBO Max’s films and TV programmes will be available as an additional tier within Netflix.

Either way, Netflix has said it intends to maintain both brands rather than subsuming HBO Max into its own service.

Netflix’s deal to take control of Warner Bros has triggered global competition concerns. The US department of justice has also launched a review to assess whether the deal would grant Netflix monopoly power in the streaming market.

Bosses are trying to reassure regulators while also fending off a rival $108bn bid tabled by Paramount, the US media group controlled by Oracle billionaire Larry Ellison. Paramount argues that its approach offers greater certainty.

While Warner Bros has repeatedly rebuffed Paramount’s overtures, the board is reportedly now considering reopening talks with the rival bidder amid mounting challenges to Netflix’s bid.

Any such move could force Netflix to increase its offer, reigniting the bidding war.

While Netflix is framing its pitch to regulators around consumer costs, the monthly price of an ad-free Netflix subscription has risen by more than 60pc since 2019, from £7.99 to £12.99.

Mr Sarandos has refused to rule out further price rises. However, he has argued that the fact that subscribers can cancel with “one click of the button” means costs will be kept in check.

HBO Max will be the new home of TNT Sports in the UK, which has the rights to some Premier League and FA Cup matches. Subscribers will have to pay an additional £30.99 per month for TNT.

The launch means Sky will lose its exclusive access to HBO programming in the UK. Sky is seeking to counter this threat with a new package that will bundle Netflix, HBO Max, Disney+ and Hayu into a single subscription costing £24 a month.

Netflix and Warner Bros declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: BAE chief demands ‘clarity’ over defence spending amid MoD-Reeves row
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s biggest defence company has called for urgent “clarity” on Britain’s military spending plans amid a Government row over budgets.

Charles Woodburn, chief executive of BAE Systems, said businesses can deliver a surge of new equipment but required “a clear signal of what’s needed” first.

The Telegraph revealed on Wednesday that Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, is pushing back against demands from the Ministry of Defence (MoD) for more money, days after Sir Keir Starmer’s pledge to build up “hard power”.

Military chiefs are said to have warned that the defence investment plan, which will set out the equipment the Government wants to buy, is currently facing a £28bn shortfall.

Ms Reeves is said to be resisting calls from the MoD for more money amid concerns that it would be wasted and require higher taxes.

Asked whether defence companies can deliver quickly if spending rises, Mr Woodburn, who is chairman of the Defence Industrial Joint Council, said: “There’s a lot of speculation as to what may or may not happen.

“But I’m actually pretty confident that the industry would be ready. We just need a clear signal or a clear steer of what’s required.

“The way we’ve stepped up in recent years, I think, is a good indication that industry can deliver when we’re given a clear outlook.

“What we’re waiting on, which I think you all know, is the defence investment plan and clarity around that.”

He added: “Earlier clarity means that the industry can make plans and invest, deploying our strong balance sheets to invest and grow in the future.

“We’re clear that we need to see the publication of this as soon as possible.”

Mr Woodburn said there was an “awful lot of work going on as we speak” to resolve the impasse on spending, “so there’s certainly no lack of effort to reach the right conclusion here”.

Fears over Tempest jet project

The delay in the defence investment plan, originally due to be published last year, has raised concerns about Britain’s next-generation fighter programme and the country’s last remaining helicopter factory.

Leonardo, the Italian defence giant that owns the former AugustaWestland plant in Somerset, has said it will be forced to shut down its manufacturing site if the MoD cannot confirm a new contract soon.

At the same time, The Telegraph this month revealed that contracts for the next phase of the Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP), a next-generation fighter project also known as Tempest, were being held up because of Britain’s delays in publishing the defence investment plan.

Sources said the holdup was causing frustration in Japan and Italy, the two other countries in the programme.

Asked about the programme on Wednesday, Mr Woodburn could not say whether GCAP would be protected from defence cuts.

He instead referred to “reassuring” remarks made by the Prime Minister recently in Tokyo and the House of Commons, saying: “At the moment, GCAP is proceeding under national contracts and the work is ongoing at pace, and I think we’re all pleased with the progress that we’re making.

“Notwithstanding, [we’re] looking for further clarity when the defence investment plan does get published.

“The PM certainly, in his comments, made some supportive remarks around GCAP.

“There’s not much more I can say, apart from going back to the PM’s reassuring comments.”

Sir Keir initially sparked speculation that he was pushing for higher spending after he told the Munich Security Conference that he wanted “more spending, faster”. However, Downing Street swiftly denied this.

Ms Reeves has been urged by some in Labour to change her “fiscal rules” to accommodate more military expenditure.

However, gilt traders separately warned the Financial Times on Wednesday that such a “ruse” would be likely to prompt investors to demand higher borrowing rates – defeating the entire purpose.

BAE boosts orders

In the meantime, it has led to criticism from the defence industry that Britain is failing to push forward with major contracts while other European countries – most notably Germany and Poland – ramp up spending.

BAE also announced two major export sales on Wednesday: Type 26 frigates to Norway and Typhoon fighter jets to Turkey.

The company ended 2025 with a record £83.6bn order backlog, up from £77.8bn the previous year, following new orders from Turkey and Norway for Typhoon fighter jets and Type 26 frigates, respectively.

Last year, it took in more than £36bn of orders globally as European countries scrambled to rearm in the face of Russian aggression and fears that Moscow will use a proposed peace deal as an opportunity to rebuild its military.

British defence officials have repeatedly warned about the possibility of a conflict with Russia in the coming years, saying that rapid rearmament is crucial.

Air Chief Marshal Sir Richard Knighton, chief of the defence staff, last week also called for “hard choices” on spending as he warned Russia’s military posture had “shifted decisively westward.”

He is understood to have told the Prime Minister about the possible £28bn spending shortfall in a Downing Street meeting before Christmas.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Hinkley Point C’s ‘fish disco’ actually works, researchers find
data.description:
An underwater “fish disco” at Britain’s newest nuclear power station has been mocked as an example of the huge cost and bureaucracy involved with building things in the UK.

But at least the new system, designed to preserve fish species, actually works.

EDF, the developer behind Hinkley Point C plant in Somerset, on Tuesday said trials of the acoustic fish deterrent – underwater speakers that blast out sound to keep fish away, dubbed the “fish disco” – found it was “highly effective”.

Tests at sea and in laboratory tanks by scientists at Swansea University showed that only a single tagged twait shad came within 98 feet of the device when it was switched on, compared to 14 when it was off.

This suggested “an effectiveness of more than 90pc”, EDF said, although further trials will be carried out later this year.

The fish disco forms part of £700m of fish protection measures planned for the nuclear power plant, which EDF said gave the site more marine conservation capability than any comparable site in the world.

The findings by Swansea University were welcomed by activists, who said the results proved that ministers should not water down environmental standards for nuclear power.

This week, a cross-party group of some 60 backbench MPs, including more than 30 from the Labour benches, joined nature charities to warn that any relaxation of standards would trigger “unnecessary and hugely damaging environmental regression”.

However, they are being opposed by pro-growth campaigners and industry experts who have pointed to a government review that found nuclear developers faced a “fragmented” regulatory system that often imposed “disproportionate” demands with little regard to costs or trade-offs.

Hinkley’s cooling pipes will suck in around 132,000 litres of water per second from the Severn Estuary to cool the plant’s two nuclear reactors once they are operational.

According to the Environment Agency, this risks putting more than four million fish at risk of being killed. These include Atlantic salmon, herring, European sea bass and European eels, which are critically endangered.

To mitigate the plant’s impact, EDF said it had spent £700m on fish protection measures, including a special intake pipe mouth costing £500m, a fish recovery and return system for £150m and an “acoustic fish deterrent” costing another £50m.

The French state-owned energy giant said the effectiveness of these measures meant it should not have to also create a 900-acre salt-marsh nearby.

Critics of the fish protection measures at Hinkley have branded them “regulatory madness”, arguing they have added huge costs and delays to the project for disproportionately small benefits.

A review by the economist John Fingleton found the fish protection measures would save “0.083 salmon per year” along with “0.028 sea trout, six river lamprey, 18 Allis shad, and 528 twaite shad”.

Sam Richards, of pro-growth campaign group Britain Remade, said: “Blowing £700m on fish protection for Hinkley Point C, including the now-legendary ‘fish disco’, is indefensible in the extreme.

“This is just the latest piece of research that highlights the huge costs and delays Britain’s planning system and habit regulations foist on major infrastructure projects, for little gain.”

Mr Richards also pointed to findings by Swansea University that salmon identified as at risk in fact avoid the Hinkley site.

Salmon migrating to the Atlantic generally used the main channel “well away from Hinkley Point C”, researchers said. In two years, only two tagged salmon were detected within a kilometre of the intake pipes.

Mr Richards said: “After years of delay and hundreds of millions spent, we’re told fish barely go near the intakes at Hinkley anyway. This isn’t environmentalism, it’s regulatory madness.”

However, Matt Browne, of the Wildlife Trusts, defended the measures and accused opponents of misrepresenting the number of fish saved.

Mr Browne said: “We are happy with the acoustic fish deterrent, combined with the other two measures.

“What we would like to see is for EDF to stop belly-aching. This is a hugely important stretch of water for fish, including protected species, and yet there have been persistent suggestions that somehow this is all unnecessary.”

Some 50,000 endangered eels alone were at risk of being killed each year without the measures, Mr Browne said.

He added: “We say this is good news – it is effective mitigation and an example of the rules working as they should do.”

Ministers are expected to set out plans for simplifying nuclear regulations in the coming weeks.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Miliband undermines Reeves’s promise to cut energy bills by £150
data.description:
Rachel Reeves’s promise to deliver a £150 reduction in household energy bills is being undercut by Ed Miliband’s net zero grid upgrades.

Analysts at Cornwall Insight have forecast that bills will fall by just £117 when the UK energy price cap is updated in April.

The Chancellor pledged a £150 cut in last November’s Budget. She said: “I can tell you today that for every family we are keeping our promise ... to get energy bills down with £150 cut from the average household bill from April next year. Money off bills and in the pockets of working people. That is my choice.”

Cornwall Insight said the reduction would be lower than promised because of spending on grid upgrades.

The Government has decided to approve £90bn of net zero upgrades to the UK’s high-voltage transmission grid by 2031 and a further £22bn on the lower-voltage distribution network that carries power from the transmission grid into buildings.

The spending is being driven forward by Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, who has set an ambitious target to decarbonise the country’s electricity grid by 2030.

Critics have said the Energy Secretary is driving too fast towards net zero, imposing unnecessary costs on consumers and businesses. The Tony Blair Institute last week urged the Government to focus more on affordability.

Ms Reeves and Mr Miliband are understood to have clashed over the Chancellor’s decision to move some green levies off bills in order to lower costs for households.

Cornwall Insight said Ms Reeves’s decision to move 75pc of the renewables obligation costs from household energy bills into general taxation and to terminate the energy company obligation levy from March could have cut bills by £145 a year.

However, it added that “increases in the charges associated with the operation and maintenance of the country’s energy networks have offset part of these savings”.

Craig Lowrey, of Cornwall Insight, said “any reduction in bills is positive, easing pressure at a time when affordability really matters” – but he also warned that upgrading the grid could add more to bills in future.

He said: “The real test will be keeping those savings going. That won’t be easy as the UK continues to upgrade its networks and infrastructure.

“That investment is needed if we want an energy system that is more secure and resilient after the consequences of exposure to global energy markets were made all too apparent in recent years.

“However, there needs to be an open conversation about the fact that such a transition will not be cost‑free.”

Cornwall Insight, which monitors the many factors that make up the average bill, expects the price cap to fall by £117 to £1,641 a year for a typical dual-fuel household. This would represent a decrease of 7pc from the current price cap, set at £1,758 per year.

The forecast coincided with new data from the UK Health Security Agency showing that cold weather caused 2,544 deaths between November 2024 and January 2025, mostly amongst older people.

The worst period, a six-day stretch in early January, saw 1,630 deaths.

High energy costs mean many people on pensions cut back on heating.

In 2020, the price cap was set at £1,042 but this rose sharply because of the energy crisis triggered by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. By October 2023, the price cap had reached £2,380.

It has declined since but with no sign it will drop back to 2020 levels.

Energy debts and arrears have soared from £1.3bn in early 2020 to around £4.5bn last September, the latest figures from Ofgem show.

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “This government is delivering on our promise to take an average of £150 of costs off bills from 1 April. Ofgem will set out the final price cap figure in the usual way next week. 

“We are also reversing decades of underinvestment in our electricity network, which will be essential to keep the lights on and ensure energy security for our country. Without these plans, which were first set out under the previous government, costs would spiral and our security would be compromised.”

data.title: Leasehold Reform Act: Everything you need to know
data.description:
Navigating the leasehold system, its confusing legalese, and bias in favour of the freeholder can be a nightmarish experience for the millions of leaseholders in the UK.

Which is why reforms that began under a Tory leadership, but will be implemented by Sir Keir Starmer’s Labour government, promising leaseholders more rights, power and protection over their homes are a welcome relief – despite the eight years it has taken for them to become law.

Running to 246 pages, the act is no easy read. However, worry not, as our guide will tell you everything you need to know about the Leasehold Reform Act and what it means for leasehold homeowners.

  • Leasehold Reform Act 2024
  • What are the implications for leaseholds?
  • When will the Leasehold Reform Act come into force?
  • When to take action as a leaseholder
  • Leasehold Reform Act FAQs

Leasehold Reform Act 2024

Making it easier, cheaper and quicker to extend a lease term, take over the management of the building or buy your freehold – these are the principles at the heart of the Leasehold Reform Act.

Here’s a summary of the measures it lays out to bring that about:

  • Standard lease extension terms will be increased to 990 years for houses and flats, from 50 and 90 years respectively.
  • Marriage value – a method of calculating the cost of a lease extension when a term falls below 80 years, which drives the bill up by thousands of pounds – will be replaced with a fairer method that is intended to make the process cheaper.
  • Leaseholders will no longer be liable to pay freeholders’ costs when they extend their lease, buy out the freehold or challenge unfair charges or poor practice at the tribunal.
  • Freeholders who manage their building themselves must belong to a redress scheme giving leaseholders a way to challenge poor practice.
  • Managing agents and freeholders must provide leaseholders with standardised service charge bills to allow leaseholders to compare and challenge costs more easily.
  • Leaseholders will no longer have to own their home for two years before being allowed to extend the term of their lease.
  • A standardised online lease extension calculator will be developed to reduce the reliance on costly valuations and create consistency.
  • More leaseholders living in blocks with commercial premises in situ will be allowed to group together to take over the management of their building – known as “Right to Manage” – as the restriction on the commercial element of a block allowed to claim this right is raised from 25pc up to 50pc.
  • A maximum time and fee will be placed on retrieving leasehold information when properties are being bought and sold.
  • The sale of new leasehold houses is banned, except in exceptional circumstances.
  • Buildings insurance commissions must be fair and transparent.

What are the implications for leaseholders?

Once the reforms are implemented, leasehold should be a fairer and more transparent system for homeowners.

One of the most significant changes, said Rob Stevens, Nationwide’s head of property risk, is the standardisation of service charge bills.

“Where leasehold gets a bad reputation is around service charges, so having that transparency is really important,” he said. “I often say to people, a high service charge doesn’t necessarily mean a bad thing, it may mean that your building is just being well maintained.

“That’s sometimes better than a low service charge, which could mean that issues are being held off and you’re getting a big bill at some time in the future. But if the bill is standardised, you’ll know exactly how your cash is being spent and weigh up if it’s value for money.”

Martin Boyd, chairman of charity Leasehold Knowledge Partnership, said changes to the rules at property tribunals has major implications for leaseholders.

“Currently, there are no circumstances in which a leaseholder has the right to recover their legal costs if they go to the tribunal.

“Landlords are always guaranteed to get some or all of their legal costs back when they’re disputing the fact they’ve done nothing wrong.

“In the future, when the secondary legislation comes forward, there’s the intention to allow leaseholders the right to make a claim for their legal costs.”

And for leaseholders with less than 80 years remaining on their lease, which can make a property difficult to sell and affect your home’s value, extending the lease should become cheaper – although the way it will be calculated has yet to be agreed on.

When will the Leasehold Reform Act come into force?

The act became law in May 2024, but the bulk of the measures have yet to be implemented.

To quote the Government, it will happen “as quickly as possible” while “taking the necessary time to ensure that reforms are watertight”.

The act itself lays out the overall framework of reforms, but that must be supported by a series of secondary legislation which provides the detail of how the reforms work in practice. While some measures have already been consulted on, such as buildings insurance fees, others have yet to be brought forward.

However, two changes have been made. Those with up to 50pc commercial premises in their building can now choose their own management company and, as of January 31, homeowners can extend their leases immediately instead of having to wait until they’ve owned the property for two years.

A group of freeholders objecting to leasehold reform were defeated in the High Court in October. They claimed that reform breached human rights and challenged the the abolishment of the current method of calculating the cost of lease extension when the term is below 80 years – known as marriage value. The Court dismissed the case in a win for the Government.

Critics have said that delays in implementation have continued after the case was settled. Harry Scoffin, of campaign group Free Leaseholders, has criticised Labour “for allowing the 2024 Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act to sit rusting”.

In January, the Government announced that ground rents – an annual fee leaseholders must pay to their freeholder – would be capped at £250. The reforms have been published in a draft of the Leasehold and Commonhold Reform Bill. This is separate Labour legislation designed to address any problems in the 2024 Act.

When to take action as a leaseholder

Given the amount of time that has passed since homeowners were promised a fairer leasehold system in the Conservative’s 2017 housing white paper, it’s difficult to say how much longer it will take before the cost of a lease extension falls.

When to take action, said Mr Boyd, depends on your own circumstances.

“If your lease has more than 80 years left to run and you don’t have ground rent giving you problems with the mortgage company, don’t do anything at the moment, wait until the legislation goes through.

“If you’ve got no option because your ground rent is too high and you can’t sell the property because no one can get a mortgage on it, then you shouldn’t wait because you’ve got no choice.”

But if your lease is under 80 years, waiting for the legislation is a gamble, said Mr Boyd, because the outcome of the judicial process and the way lease extension premiums will be calculated is not yet known.

“If you were being in any way cautious, you’d probably want to extend your lease now because you don’t know what the results are going to be in the future.”

Leasehold Reform Act FAQs

You may have the opportunity to buy the freehold of your property, but you will first need to find out if you are eligible to do so.

Lease, the leasehold advisory service, says that to buy the freehold of a house, you will need to meet certain criteria:

  • If the house has been converted into flats, you will need to have the lease for the entire building
  • When it was granted, the lease must have been for more than 21 years
  • You must be the leaseholder. Prior to January 2025 you had to have held the lease for two years – but this rule has since been abolished.

If you would like to buy the freehold of a block of flats, leaseholders can group together to buy the freehold. You would then own a “share of freehold”.

It is then a case of working out how much it will cost and instructing lawyers. The Telegraph’s guide takes you through the steps.

The majority of the measures in the act apply to Wales, however Wales will work to its own timescale in creating subordinate legislation to implement the measures.

data.title: Ed Miliband plots crackdown on wind farms ‘gaming’ power system
data.description:
Wind farm owners who earn huge payouts from gaming Britain’s energy system face a crackdown under new reforms proposed by power grid bosses.

The National Energy System Operator (Neso) warned on Wednesday that companies were deliberately clogging up Britain’s energy system to boost their own revenues.

This is because wind farms can be paid to switch off when the grid becomes too congested to transport their power, allowing traders to game the system.

Neso, which handles the day-to-day operations of the electricity grid, said it was concerned about the sale of power generated by Scottish wind farms to buyers in the South, even when traders know that grid congestion means the power physically cannot be delivered.

There is currently no penalty for doing this, but it means Neso must constantly intervene to balance the system.

For example, it will pay Scottish wind farms to switch off while also paying another power station in the South to generate replacement electricity.

Under the current model, the wind farm and the power station could also be owned by the same company, allowing that company to distort supply to earn payments.

Neso warned the system had created an incentive for companies “to aggravate the constraint” as this “maximises the market participant’s expected revenue”.

It now wants to force generators to sell power from individual power stations, making it more obvious when the system is being gamed.

It also wants to require generators to settle their plans further in advance to minimise last-minute changes to the system.

Balancing costs, which can easily amount to millions of pounds per day, are ultimately passed on to households through their bills.

The problem threatens to push up household bills in a blow to Ed Miliband’s promise to cut them by £300 a year.

Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, last year scrapped reforms that supporters said would have ended such market gaming practices following an intense lobbying campaign by wind farm owners, who argued it would torpedo investment.

The amount spent on balancing the grid hit £2.7bn last year alone and is expected to reach between £5bn and £8bn a year by 2030 without action to tackle the problem, according to Neso.

Neso warned: “The misalignment between the national wholesale price and locational balancing needs, along with the scale and predictability of congestion, presents significant risks of strategic positioning against constraints.

“This risk is more likely to arise if the network becomes more congested.”

RenewableUK, which represents wind farm owners, claimed the problem affected “all types of power generation”.

A Neso report last year said most constraint costs were focused in Scotland, where Britain’s wind farms are most concentrated.

A RenewableUK spokesman added: “The industry is working closely with the Government to ensure that innovative solutions are rolled out at speed, such as new energy storage capacity, to add more flexibility into the system”.

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “After years of historic underinvestment, we are delivering the biggest upgrade in Great Britain’s electricity network in decades to minimise constraint costs, delivering clean power by 2030 and helping bring down bills for good.

“We are working closely with Neso, Ofgem and industry to further improve the system as part of our Reformed National Pricing package.”

data.title: Crippling energy prices show folly of Miliband’s California deal
data.description:
When Steve Hilton worked as a barefooted senior aide to David Cameron in the early 2010s, he was credited as the man who turned the Tories green.

Times have changed. Now, the one-time Downing Street svengali is a would-be Republican candidate to replace Democrat Gavin Newsom as California governor in the election this November – and Britain’s climate policies make him see red.

Hilton vented his anger on X after Newsom struck a clean energy agreement this week with Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

“What a genius idea that is: the UK, with the highest electricity prices pretty much in the world, teaming up with California, the highest electricity prices anywhere in America apart from Hawaii,” he said in a video.

Hilton said the deal would have “a very short shelf life” – he would “rip it up” if he won the governorship on Nov 3.

“We are going to have affordable, reliable energy in California because I’m going to end this insane climate crusade,” he said.

Hilton was giving voice to a groundswell of anger and frustration in California over the state’s punishing electricity prices.

Californian households were paying 32 cents (23p) per kilowatt hour for electricity at the end of last year, according to the US Energy Information Administration. That was almost double the US national average of just under 18 cents.

The same disparity afflicts California’s industrial users of electricity: they paid 19 cents against a national average of 8.4 cents.

The average electricity price paid by a residential consumer nationwide rose 22pc between 2020 and 2024 – but at the three biggest Californian utilities, the price increase was between 31pc and 70pc.

Hilton and others on the Right blame Newsom’s aggressive net zero policies for fuelling this above-average increase in power costs.

Criticising Labour’s decision to strike a deal with California, Donald Trump said: “Gavin is a loser. Everything he’s touched turns to garbage. His state has gone to hell and his environmental work is a disaster.

“If they did to the UK what he did to California, this will not be a very successful venture.”

Newsom, who is widely seen as a potential Democratic presidential candidate, has been governor of California since 2019.

When he came to office, the state had a target to reduce emissions to 40pc below a 1990 benchmark by 2030. In 2022, the target was ratcheted up to 85pc with net zero lined up for 2045.

To get there, electricity providers have to ensure that an ever-increasing share of generation comes from renewable sources with a target of 90pc for 2030.

The programme has cut annual greenhouse gas emissions from the electricity sector by 40pc in a decade – but the cost has been high.

Nick Chaset, the boss of British retailer Octopus Energy’s US operation, told The Telegraph last year: “California is very much net zero or nothing. It’s about trying to construct a perfect world, [which means] it becomes extremely expensive.”

Chaset on Tuesday said net-zero and affordable bills did not have to be “mutually exclusive”. He said Miliband’s deal with Newsom was “an example of how governments with aggressive climate policies can directly address the practical challenges of delivering net zero while managing consumer costs”.

But in Britain, Miliband has faced criticism similar to Hilton’s. Detractors say his target of decarbonising Britain’s electricity grid by 2030 is too aggressive, imposing needless costs on the country solely in the pursuit of hitting an arbitrary goal.

Wayne Winegarden, of the Pacific Research Institute, told the New York Post this week that California was prioritising net zero over “key aspects of affordability and reliability”.

“We rely on more of what we want to be true – or even what we think could possibly be true in a few years – rather than what’s true today,” he said.

One of the main drivers behind those fast-growing bills has been the requirement for Californian electricity generators to race towards renewables.

The California Legislative Analyst’s Office (LAO), the state government’s independent budgetary watchdog, has reported that this had already added 5pc to electricity bills by 2020.

Initially, that was because the providers’ investment and generation costs were higher than from existing plants – but even as the cost of renewables fell, the LAO said the operators had to keep spending more. This was because the renewable energy was often generated in remote areas, requiring new or upgraded transmission lines.

The energy is also often more intermittent than fossil fuel-powered supplies. This means natural gas plants, for example, have to be kept online and on standby, incurring an extra cost. Investment in battery storage also had to be stepped up.

Household bills then had another 4pc a year on average tacked on through new “public purpose charges”. These charges fund programmes such as expanding the charging network for electric vehicles or developing energy efficiency measures.

Perversely, another cause of rising bills for many households has emerged from California’s embrace of rooftop solar.

In 2014, households using the state’s three largest electricity providers got only 2pc of their electricity collectively from their own rooftops. By 2024, that share had climbed to 20pc.

Suppliers’ fixed costs for feeding grid power to the households remain the same even as demand falls. As more people switch to free electricity from rooftop solar, a shrinking number of households are left paying for the providers’ fixed costs.

“As fewer kilowatt hours are sold from the grid, retail rates must rise even more in order to recover the fixed costs of the system,” says Severin Borenstein, a UC Berkeley professor.

He estimates that in 2024 the take-up of rooftop solar shifted almost $4bn (£3bn) on to the bills of those who didn’t have the panels.

There are other reasons why California’s bills have outpaced the rest of America’s, too.

The biggest is the cost of protecting and insuring the network against the state’s increasingly frequent and destructive wildfires.

Seven years ago, householders did not help meet this cost. Now, almost 15pc of their electricity bills goes towards countering this threat.

In some respects, the higher electricity bills help Newsom’s net zero agenda – householders are incentivised to use electricity less or use it more efficiently – but try telling that to the Californian households who can’t run their aircon in a sweltering heatwave.

The higher prices also hit industrial consumers who fuel the state’s economy plus the Silicon Valley users of energy-hungry data centres.

In a report last year, the LAO put this trade-off into typically bureaucratic language for Newsom and his officials.

“Key decision: how to balance the state’s ambitious greenhouse gas reduction goals against the inevitable costs that will result for ratepayers,” it said.

Tom Manzo, the founder of the California Business and Industrial Alliance, was more blunt. Speaking to US media, he blamed Newsom’s “over-regulation and anti-business business climate” for driving up energy costs and hurting companies.

“You’re not helping the state of California by going and making some made-up deal with somebody from the United Kingdom,” he said.

Some of Miliband’s own detractors might well agree.

data.title: Builder denies affair cost him slice of mother’s £2.5m fortune
data.description:
A builder was all but excluded from his late mother’s fortune partly because she was “ashamed” of him for cheating on his wife, a court has heard.

Gary MacDougall, 70, had expected to split Jeanne MacDougall’s estate – valued at £2.5m – with his sister Sandra Thomas, 65, and her husband, Lloyd, after she died in April 2020.

But because of a change to her will in 2011 and a series of property sales and gifts, Mr MacDougall is likely to be left with “nil”, the High Court was told.

He is now suing at the High Court to overturn his mother’s last will, while also accusing his sister and brother-in-law of plundering her fortune while she was still alive but incapable of consent.

At a hearing this week, he was forced to deny that his mother had genuine reasons to cut him out, including her shame over him cheating on his wife, Anna, with another woman.

Alexander Learmonth KC, for the Thomases, told the court there were valid reasons why Mrs MacDougall had changed her will in favour of her daughter and son-in-law.

He said the couple had cared for her in her old age, whereas Mr MacDougall, who had worked with his mother in the family business, had a “fractious” relationship with her.

He told the court: “Like all testators, Jeanne clearly had a variety of reasons for wanting to make a new will in the terms she did.

“To some extent, it would be speculation as to what they were, and the court is not required to indulge in that, but they certainly included a sense of how well-off Gary had become, and gratitude for the time and care given her by [Lloyd] and Sandra.

“They may also have included growing irritation with Gary, whether that be his marital infidelity, sharp words in the office, infrequency of visits or otherwise. It certainly cannot be said that the 2011 will is in any way irrational.”

Cross-examining Mr MacDougall, the barrister suggested that his mother, as a member of an older, more traditional generation, was “disappointed” in her son about his affair in 2008.

“Your mother, being of the older generation and being fond of your wife, was not happy with you, was she?” he put to Mr MacDougall. “She was very disappointed with what you had done.”

Mr MacDougall replied: “She wasn’t happy, no – she was delighted when it concluded.

“She reminded me of my responsibilities, but it was a very brief fling. If she was cross with me, Mum would have let me know. She would have given me both barrels.”

Mr Learmonth claimed that Mrs MacDougall, when making her will, was also conscious of the benefits her son had already received, having ultimately become sole owner of the family business.

He had received “huge assets” and now has “a considerable portfolio of rental properties, as well as a holiday home in Cyprus and an oast house in the countryside,” he said.

Mr MacDougall insisted that his success was down to him “grafting” while his sister and brother-in-law lived “the life of luxury”, and added: “Where’s the reward?”

Family fortune built up in property

Judge Nicola Rushton KC heard the MacDougall family fortune derived from the “substantial real estate portfolio” built up ty the siblings’ father Alec, a property developer.

Harry Martin, Mr MacDougall’s barrister, said that over the years it had been made clear to the two siblings by their parents that they would ultimately receive “broadly equal financial treatment and inheritance”.

After their father’s death, the siblings’ mother made a will in 2008, which Mr Martin said amounted to a “broadly equal” split between her son and his family and her daughter and son-in-law.

But another will was then made in 2011, under which all of her four properties went to Mrs Thomas and her husband, and they received the majority of her savings.

Mr MacDougall and his sister would split the small amount that was left, but after the costs and expenses of estate administration, that is “likely to be worth nil”, the court was told.

Mr MacDougall claims the will is invalid because of “presumed undue influence” having been made at a time when his mother was elderly and dependent on his sister and brother-in-law.

‘More than £2m left bank accounts’

He is also laying claim to a share of about £1.7m of his mother’s money which he says was “misappropriated” from her bank accounts before she died, and spent by his sister and brother-in-law on themselves and their family.

Mr Martin said more than £2m left Mrs MacDougall’s bank accounts between 2012 and 2020, with less than £500,000 being attributed to spending for her own benefit.

Alleged expenditure by Mrs Thomas and her husband included “paying for their daughter’s wedding at the five-star Savoy Hotel in London, shopping on Oxford Street, flights, spending on holiday in Spain, £30,000 to the car manufacturer Jaguar and meals out at The Ivy”, the barrister said.

Mr Learmonth accepted Mrs Thomas and her husband exceeded their authority under the power of attorney, but did not do so deliberately, having misunderstood what they were entitled to do.

The case continues.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Florence flats so ugly they’ve triggered a police investigation
data.description:
Not far from the banks of the Arno River, an ultra-modern block of upmarket flats nicknamed the Black Cube casts a gloomy shadow over the butter and cream-coloured villas lining the embankment.

“Brutto [ugly],” says resident Stefano Pieri, shaking his head as he peered across the river at the dark, burnished brass facade of the building in the heart of Florence. “Some might say it is beautiful modern architecture when it stands alone. The problem is it is completely out of context here.”

The building is indeed out of place – so much so it is now subject to a police investigation, dragging in everyone from architects to councillors. Even the city’s nobility have intervened, breaking cover to stop any further developments from spoiling a skyline that has changed little in hundreds of years.

The Black Cube – officially called the Teatro Luxury Apartments – is a stone’s throw from the US embassy and the Santa Maria Novella train station, and includes smart restaurants, spas and underground parking. The modern palazzo, managed by the Star Hotels chain, offers 150 short-term rental units for high-income students and professionals.

Florentine prosecutors are now questioning 12 people who are under investigation for irregularities in the permitting, planning and zoning process that paved the way for the Black Cube to rise high above the former site of the Teatro Comunale. They are trying to trace how the property was decommissioned and sold in 2013 by the then Matteo Renzi city administration.

What they know so far is that the property passed through a state entity then was auctioned off to an Italian investment subsidiary. From there it was sold to a joint venture formed by property giants Blue Noble and Hines, within the Future Living fund managed by London-based Savills. Hines maintains it constructed the building with all the necessary permits in place.

Here in the centre of the Renaissance city of the 14th-century Palazzo Vecchio, Giotto’s Campanile and Brunelleschi’s Dome, residents cannot even change the shade of their window shutters without an extensive bureaucratic permit process.

People are now questioning just how a US property developer was granted permission to build something locals also refer to as The Monster of Corso Italia.

A powerful group of ancient noble families has called for a moratorium on further high-end developments down river from the Black Cube in a former industrial area near the former Leopolda railway station.

In a letter to the mayor, the 16 noble families said: “We must save Florence from any other ‘black cubes’. No more violence toward our city.” Some of the letter’s signatory families have owned property in the city since the Medici era (1434 to 1737).

Convincing them to speak out has been called Operation Blue Blood by local media, and is largely thanks to a lobbying effort by Roberto Budini Gattai, an architect nicknamed the Communist Nobleman, who has been at the forefront of the protests.

According to Italian media, the letter of protest was drafted over an elegant lunch, where the members of the ancient families finally agreed to sign up to the cause. Among the signatories is Claude Marie Agnès Cathérine d’Orléans, 82, a French princess and former Duchess of Aosta, who married Prince Amedeo of Savoy in 1964 and has lived in Florence ever since.

The Princess is not one to mince her words. “The Black Cube and the Social Hub on Viale Belfiore are monstrosities,” she told the Corriere Fiorentino. “I apologise to the architects who designed them, but that’s the way it is. I don’t understand why, when you do something new, you don’t try – I don’t mean copying – but perhaps taking inspiration from older buildings.”

It is the question on everyone’s lips here. “Its obviously not for Florentines,” said Jacopo Palorni, 37, as he walked along the riverbank with his wife and toddler in a stroller.

Palorni and Maria Christina Silvestre are among many residents who have been forced to move out of the historic centre of Florence and find somewhere more affordable on the outskirts. Like many, they feel their concerns about the urban future of their city go mostly unheeded. They said: “When they began construction we sent several emails, but never got any response.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Boohoo races to raise £35m to ease balance sheet pressure
data.description:
Boohoo has unveiled plans to raise £35m and renegotiate loans to ease pressures on its balance sheet.

On Tuesday, the online retailer said it was holding talks with shareholders over a fresh cash-raise, as well as speaking to lenders about loosening debt terms.

The company, which rebranded as Debenhams last year, said the steps would provide it with “greater financial flexibility to deliver its turnaround and associated growth plan”. It is expected to help bring its debt interest costs down around £20m a year.

It comes as part of a drive by bosses to turn around the ailing fashion retailer, which has been struggling with fierce competition from the cheap, fast-fashion rival Shein. It plunged to a £348m loss last year, with sales tumbling almost a fifth to £790m. Dan Finley, the brand’s chief executive, admitted it was an “unacceptable” performance from the group.

More recently, executives have been battling to slash the company’s debt pile amid concerns about the strain on its balance sheet.

It had been planning to sell fashion brand PrettyLittleThing as part of a push to cut debt levels, but pulled the sale late last month, saying the brand’s performance had improved.

However, investors have remained concerned over its balance sheet. On Tuesday morning, shares in Boohoo were down 10pc, bringing them more than a fifth below last year’s levels.

At the end of its last financial year, Boohoo revealed its net assets collapsed to just £3.9m from £280m a year earlier, while net debt stood at £111m at the end of August 2025. Last year, it agreed to a £175m debt package.

Analysts at Peel Hunt said the latest update showed that Boohoo was “clearly bumping up against its debt covenants” on the £175m loan deal.

The arrangement had previously been the subject of a row with Boohoo’s largest shareholder, Frasers, which questioned the loan deal’s high interest rate. The debt carries an interest rate of 7.3pc above the Bank of England base rate.

Writing to Tim Morris, Boohoo’s chairman, last year, Frasers said: “How is it in the best interests of Boohoo, its shareholders and its other stakeholders for so much money to be sucked out of the Boohoo group when cheaper financing was available?”

The criticism follows years of tussles between Boohoo and Mike Ashley’s retail empire, Frasers. Mr Ashley attempted to oust Mahmud Kamani, Boohoo’s co-founder, from the company’s board, as well as two other members of the Boohoo leadership team, last August.

In November, Boohoo blocked Mr Ashley from voting on a £150m bonus scheme for its chief executive, Mr Finley. It claimed that a “major competitor” and a “significant shareholder” had set out to “disrupt Debenhams Group’s growth strategy and operations” by voting against a series of measures at past shareholder meetings.

Frasers is understood not to be blocked from the new fundraising round.

Boohoo said Mr Kamani, its executive vice chairman, would take part in the £35m fundraising, along with Mr Finley, its chief executive.

In an update after confirming the fundraising plans, Boohoo said it had received “indicative support” to raise £24m from investors. “The company will continue speaking to its institutional shareholders over the next few days to gauge further indicative support,” Boohoo said.

Management added that the company expected performance to “materially improve”.

Writing to Tim Morris, Boohoo’s chairman, last year, Frasers said: “How is it in the best interests of Boohoo, its shareholders and its other stakeholders for so much money to be sucked out of the Boohoo group when cheaper financing was available?”

The criticism follows years of tussles between Boohoo and Mike Ashley’s retail empire, Frasers. Mr Ashley attempted to oust Mahmud Kamani, Boohoo’s co-founder, from the company’s board, as well as two other members of the Boohoo leadership team, last August.

In November, Boohoo blocked Mr Ashley from voting on a £150m bonus scheme for its chief executive, Mr Finley. It claimed that a “major competitor” and a “significant shareholder” had set out to “disrupt Debenhams Group’s growth strategy and operations” by voting against a series of measures at past shareholder meetings.

Frasers is understood not to be blocked from the new fundraising round.

Boohoo said Mr Kamani, its executive vice chairman, would take part in the £35m fundraising, along with Mr Finley, its chief executive.

In an update after confirming the fundraising plans, Boohoo said it had received “indicative support” to raise £24m from investors. “The company will continue speaking to its institutional shareholders over the next few days to gauge further indicative support,” Boohoo said.

Management added that the company expected performance to “materially improve”.

data.title: Gail’s boss: ‘Mood among employers is darkest I’ve ever seen’
data.description:
The co-owner of Gail’s Bakery has warned the mood of UK employers is the “darkest” he has ever seen as surging minimum wages price young people out of the jobs market.

Luke Johnson, a key financial backer of the chain for over a decade, criticised ministers on Wednesday for making it “more expensive and more risky to employ people” after companies were hit with fresh red tape and wage hikes.

“No one in this Cabinet has ever been in a business, none of them have ever created any jobs, and I’m afraid to say there is a bigger disconnect between this Government and the business people I know than any government I’ve ever come across,” he told the BBC.

Mr Johnson added: “I’ve spent 40 years growing businesses and creating jobs, and the mood amongst employers and those who invest in this country is the darkest I’ve ever seen.”

Mr Johnson, a serial entrepreneur who also previously chaired PizzaExpress, also said he did not believe Labour had “ever listened to business”.

The comments come after figures this week revealed that youth unemployment hit an 11-year high, with almost one in six people aged 18 to 24 currently out of work, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS).

The alarming figures are said to have prompted a rethink by ministers amid fears recent policies are fuelling a worklessness crisis.

On Tuesday, the Times reported that the Government was reviewing a manifesto pledge to increase the minimum wage for young people to bring it in line with older workers by 2029.

Already, minimum pay for those aged 21 to 22 has increased by 33pc over the past two years. In April, young workers will get another 8.5pc minimum wage increase.

Labour was ‘right to be worried’

However, retail and hospitality bosses have warned this is making it less attractive to hire younger, less experienced staff over more experienced older workers.

Allan Simpson, the chief executive of UKHospitality, said the looming minimum wage increase for younger workers will add “another strain at a moment when youth unemployment is already climbing”.

He said: “These lost opportunities for young people are deeply worrying for an industry that has always given people their first chance in the workplace.”

Mr Simpson welcomed a “more measured” approach to equalising youth and adult age rates, saying: “Slowing the process would help protect entry-level roles and keep pathways into work open for young people.”

Helen Dickinson, the chief executive of the retail industry group the British Retail Consortium, also said the Government was “right to be worried about the impact that equalising national minimum wage too quickly would have on employment”.

She said Labour should review the “cumulative impact” of its policies on the jobs market to make sure it is not “making a fragile labour market worse”.

A government spokesman said: “We’re determined to help young people into work and deliver on our manifesto commitments to make work pay. 

“That’s why we are raising the National Living and Minimum Wage so that low-paid workers are properly rewarded – worth a £1,500 boost to the lowest paid workers.

“We’re also backing businesses to hire young people by fully funding apprenticeships for SMEs [small and medium-sized enterprises] and offering relief on National Insurance contributions for businesses to employ young people.”

data.title: Millions of drivers could miss out on car finance payouts
data.description:
Victims of the car finance scandal stand to miss out on hundreds of pounds in compensation under plans that could spare carmakers from paying out.

The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) said last year that banks and carmakers would likely be on the hook for £11bn under its plans to compensate customers for mis-sold motor finance.

But Britain’s main financial regulator is preparing to backtrack on proposals that would have paid compensation if carmakers failed to disclose to customers that they had an exclusive contract with a lender or gave that lender first right of refusal, according to the Financial Times.

Around 3.2 million motorists were sold contracts where there were these “tied arrangements” in place, according to the FCA. But only loans that were provided by captive lenders – which are carmakers with their own in-house banking arm – would be affected by the exemption.

Roughly £2.1bn of the total car finance bill are related to tied arrangements, with each victim in line for an average of £686 in compensation.

Millions of drivers who signed personal contract purchase (PCP) or hire purchase (HP) contracts between April 2007 and November 2024 could be eligible for compensation because they were not told about commission arrangements or tied arrangements on loans at the point of purchase.

Around 32.5 million agreements were sold in that period, according to the FCA.

The majority of car finance mis-sold complaints concern discretionary commission arrangements, where brokers failed to disclose that they could adjust the customer’s interest rate to obtain higher commission.

Others were not told that a broker’s commission was at least 35pc of the total cost of credit and 10pc of the loan. These two other elements of the compensation scheme would not be affected by the FCA climbdown, according to the Financial Times.

The regulator started a consultation on its redress programme last October, two months after the Supreme Court found that a lender acted unlawfully because of the high, undisclosed commission paid to the broker and the failure to disclose a contractual tie.

Last year, it was revealed that Rachel Reeves had attempted to intervene in the case to protect lenders from paying large compensation bills. Ms Reeves was reportedly concerned that the case could make it harder for drivers to obtain car finance.

Following the Supreme Court’s ruling, carmakers had reportedly lobbied the FCA to exempt captive lenders from tied arrangements compensation.

They had argued that their in-house banking arms had lent at the most attractive rate for new car purchases.

Adrian Dally, of trade body Finance and Leasing Association, said: “We think there was literally no loss to consumers in that segment. Captive finance companies’ aim is to help consumers buy their manufacturer’s cars.

“The best way to get consumers to buy more cars is to have very cheap finance – low interest rates or 0pc interest rates. The captive finance company exists to provide the cheapest possible finance on the market for that car.”

An FCA spokesman said: “We’re carefully considering feedback and decisions on final scheme rules have not been taken.”

The regulator is expected to publish its finalised rules for the scheme within the next six weeks.

data.title: Brace for Trump’s brave new world of 1.7°C global warming
data.description:
If you have stopped paying attention to global warming, you may soon be in for a nasty surprise.

A paper by Columbia University predicts that we will have our first taste of a 1.7C world as soon as next year – a shock big enough to intrude on everybody’s consciousness.

One cause is the reversion from the current cooling weather pattern of La Niña in the South Pacific to the opposite El Niño pattern of cyclical warmth.

A less understood cause is that aerosol pollution has masked the latent heat effect of past CO2 emissions by around 0.5C and possibly more. Carbon lingers in the atmosphere for several hundred years. This aerosol effect is going into rapid reverse as the world cuts toxic particulates from coal plants, industry, cars and shipping.

The Columbia estimate of 1.7C above 1880-1920 levels is at the high end of the scientific spectrum but the Met Office, Europe’s Copernicus service and others also expect a record-shattering year in 2027.

The paper caught my eye because the Columbia team is led by Jim Hansen, the veteran Nasa scientist, whom I interviewed in 1987 after his watershed testimony to Congress on the greenhouse threat.

Every now and then in journalism, you talk to somebody who changes your structural view. He was one of them. He turned me into what is today an endangered species: a green conservative. Don’t all hurl shoes at once.

The warnings of climate scientists have been vindicated over the decades. The last three years have jolted even the most cautious, smashing records faster than models had predicted. A string of events have been “outside the expected envelope” from the melting of the Antarctic ice shelf to marine heatwaves in the North Atlantic.

It is the paradox of our age that anti-green backlash is sweeping the West – though not the East – just as the evidence hardens and as we move closer to runaway feedback loops that ought to scare the wits out of us.

In case you missed it, the Green Alliance has obtained a suppressed report by the Government and the joint intelligence committee that spells out the risks.

“Ecosystem degradation is occurring across all regions. Every critical ecosystem is on a pathway to collapse (irreversible loss of function beyond repair),” said the text.

Last week will surely go down as one of the most astonishing episodes in the long sorry saga of human folly. Donald Trump revoked the bedrock “endangerment finding”, the legal weapon used by the US Environmental Protection Agency to curb greenhouse emissions over the last 17 years.

The president issued an executive order commanding the US armed forces to buy power from coal-fired plants with “long-term power purchase agreements” that will extend beyond his term in office, a method known as “pendulum proofing”.

The US energy department announced in the same week that it would hand out $175m (£129m) to keep six coal plants open under wartime emergency powers. It is the latest tranche of $525m in subsidies intended to relaunch coal, which has long been in steep decline because it cannot compete with gas, solar and wind.

Trump is actively intervening against free market forces with taxpayer money to prop up the dirtiest form of fossil power.

The federal Tennessee Valley Authority said in parallel that it would reverse plans to shut down 12 of the country’s oldest coal plants. Myron Ebell, a White House adviser, said “total victory” is now close at hand.

This self-indulgent, hedonistic nihilism by the world’s hegemonic power is more than a little irritating. America’s annual per capita CO2 emissions are 14.2 tonnes compared to Japan (7.8), Germany (6.8), Italy (5.1), the UK (4.5) and France (4.0). Texas emits six times as much pro rata as France.

It is hard to gauge how much difference Trump’s provocations will make. Some 96pc of extra electricity added in the US last year came from solar and wind – but that is a lag-effect from the Joe Biden and Trump 1.0 eras.

Trump 2.0 is waging all-out war to obstruct and delay 140 gigawatts (GW) of renewable expansion, even where it receives no federal subsidy and even though data centres need all the power they can get.

However, which brave investor is going to build a new coal plant today knowing that a) the Democrats may retake Congress; b) electrotech is getting better and cheaper every year; and c) the volatile public mood could quickly turn against the fossil industry again?

Carbon Brief says the US still has 189 GW of coal plants. Three quarters are more than 40 years old and many are over 50 years old. Not a single plant has been built in 14 years.

The good news is that coal-fired power fell 3pc in India and 1.6pc in China last year, despite brisk growth in electricity demand.

“China makes almost all the windmills and yet I haven’t been able to find any wind farms in China,” said Trump in Davos. “They sell them to the stupid people that buy them but don’t use them themselves.”

He has not looked very hard. The North China Plain as far as Xinjiang is covered with turbines. China added 430 GW of wind and solar last year. Installed capacity has reached 1,800 GW, roughly 60pc of the world total.

For the record, almost none of the wind turbines in Europe and the US was made in China. Trump is confusing wind with solar panels.

Carbon Brief says China’s total emissions have plateaued over the last 15 months and fell 1pc in the final quarter of 2025.

Over half of cars sold in the country last year were electrified and that market is today as large as the US and the EU car markets combined. Electric heavy lorries are next.

China’s electrotech revolution is now the giant defining force of global energy politics. The ripple effects are visible across south-east Asia. The Chinese model is outcompeting America’s fossil model across large parts of the globe.

Europe is wobbling but it has not yet ditched plans to cut coal, gas and oil demand. Trump’s political weaponisation of energy has given the region a very strong incentive to cut even faster.

Alarmism about the climate – and I use the term in a positive sense – rises and falls in cycles. The mood is highly manipulated by vested interests using well-honed “K Street” methods of disinformation but the public also knows perfectly well that climate change is happening and poses an intolerable threat to their children.

At some point there will be a backlash against the backlash and the next one may be even bigger than the Greta Thunberg storm.

I suspect that this is already brewing as the energy stress from Vladimir Putin’s war subsides. All it requires is the catalyst of a climate shocker or two, or three, to recapture the headlines: a 1.7C world in 2027 would surely do it.

My advice to the Tories is listen hard to distant but approaching thunder. Climate negligence and fossil politics will not be a winning formula in the hotter world of the next election. That niche is best left to Reform.

There is a wide-open window for the Conservatives to be the responsible party of nature and free market, winning back the hard-working silent middle as urban Labour lurches further to the welfare Left.

Ecology will soon be fashionable again.
